The idea is that it would navigate the obsidian vault, so I can ask it things, and also edit and modify the pages always using the templates and my system for the vault. Design the necessary skills for the agent too. Web search whatecer you need to see similar solutuons. and spawn subagents for the subtasks

1. First, I need to understand the current project structure and any existing configs
2. I need to understand Oh My Pi agent/skill structure
3. I need to look at the user's Obsidian vault to understand their templates and system
4. I need to research similar solutions online

1rk|# Skills
2nd|
3cr|Skills are file-backed capability packs discovered at startup and exposed to the model as:
4th|
5sj|- lightweight metadata in the system prompt (name + description)
6ea|- on-demand content via `read skill://...`
7wl|- optional interactive `/skill:<name>` commands
8th|
9rw|This document covers current runtime behavior in `src/extensibility/skills.ts`, `src/discovery/builtin.ts`, `src/internal-urls/skill-protocol.ts`, and `src/discovery/agents-md.ts`.
10th|
11lr|## What a skill is in this codebase
12th|
13kb|A discovered skill is represented as:
14th|
15bw|- `name`
16ma|- `description`
17wi|- `filePath` (the `SKILL.md` path)
18cj|- `baseDir` (skill directory)
19tc|- source metadata (`provider`, `level`, path)
20th|
21wo|The runtime only requires `name` and `path` for validity. In practice, matching quality depends on `description` being meaningful.
22nd|
23hw|## Required layout and SKILL.md expectations
24th|
25rm|### Directory layout
26th|
27sc|For provider-based discovery (native/Claude/Codex/Agents/plugin providers), skills are discovered as **one level under `skills/`**:
28th|
29pg|- `<skills-root>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`
30th|
31jb|Nested patterns like `<skills-root>/group/<skill>/SKILL.md` are not discovered by provider loaders.
32nd|
33hl|For `skills.customDirectories`, scanning uses the same non-recursive layout (`*/SKILL.md`).
34th|
35xc|```text
36zh|Provider-discovered layout (non-recursive under skills/):
37th|
38kx|<root>/skills/
39yf|  ├─ postgres/
40dm|  │   └─ SKILL.md      ✅ discovered
41gt|  ├─ pdf/
42dm|  │   └─ SKILL.md      ✅ discovered
43jy|  └─ team/
44zz|      └─ internal/
45lc|          └─ SKILL.md  ❌ not discovered by provider loaders
46th|
47qq|Custom-directory scanning is also non-recursive, so nested paths are ignored unless you point `customDirectories` at that nested parent.
48wl|```
49th|
50kp|### `SKILL.md` frontmatter
51st|
52es|Supported frontmatter fields on the skill type:
53rd|
54yv|- `name?: string`
55av|- `description?: string`
56ex|- `globs?: string[]`
57bu|- `alwaysApply?: boolean`
58op|- additional keys are preserved as unknown metadata
59th|
60wr|Current runtime behavior:
61st|
62ps|- `name` defaults to the skill directory name
63pb|- `description` is required for:
64oi|  - native `.omp` provider skill discovery (`requireDescription: true`)
65ve|  - `skills.customDirectories` scans via `scanSkillsFromDir` in `src/discovery/helpers.ts` (non-recursive)
66yv|- non-native providers can load skills without description
67th|
68lu|## Discovery pipeline
69th|
70vy|`discoverSkills()` in `src/extensibility/skills.ts` does two passes:
71st|
72mg|1. **Capability providers** via `loadCapability("skills")`
73ag|2. **Custom directories** via `scanSkillsFromDir(..., { requireDescription: true })` (one-level directory enumeration)
74th|
75hm|If `skills.enabled` is `false`, discovery returns no skills.
76th|
77cb|### Built-in skill providers and precedence
78th|
79af|Provider ordering is priority-first (higher wins), then registration order for ties.
80th|
81qt|Current registered skill providers:
82nd|
83kt|1. `native` (priority 100) — `.omp` user/project skills via `src/discovery/builtin.ts`
84vh|2. `claude` (priority 80)
85iw|3. priority 70 group (in registration order):
86vl|   - `claude-plugins`
87jy|   - `agents`
88di|   - `codex`
89jl|4. `opencode` (priority 55)
90yf|   Dedup key is skill name. First item with a given name wins.
91st|
92ro|### Source toggles and filtering
93rd|
94gv|`discoverSkills()` applies these controls:
95th|
96ha|- source toggles: `enableCodexUser`, `enableClaudeUser`, `enableClaudeProject`, `enablePiUser`, `enablePiProject`
97jx|- `disabledExtensions` entries with `skill:<name>`
98wp|- `ignoredSkills` (exclude)
99vt|- `includeSkills` (include allowlist; empty means include all)
100th|
101qu|Filter order is:
102nd|
103us|1. not disabled by `disabledExtensions`
104ul|2. source enabled
105nl|3. not ignored
106mz|4. included (if include list present)
107av|   For providers other than codex/claude/native (for example `agents`, `claude-plugins`, `opencode`), enablement currently falls back to: enabled if **any** built-in source toggle is enabled.
108th|
109ns|### Collision and duplicate handling
110th|
111qw|- Capability dedup already keeps first skill per name (highest-precedence provider)
112xh|- `extensibility/skills.ts` additionally:
113tf|  - de-duplicates identical files by `realpath` (symlink-safe)
114xy|  - emits collision warnings when a later skill name conflicts
115sr|  - keeps the convenience `discoverSkillsFromDir({ dir, source })` API as a thin adapter over `scanSkillsFromDir`
116um|- Custom-directory skills are merged after provider skills and follow the same collision behavior
117th|
118fh|## Runtime usage behavior
119th|
120mp|### System prompt exposure
121st|
122cy|System prompt construction (`src/system-prompt.ts`) uses discovered skills as follows:
123rd|
124qp|- if `read` tool is available:
125ks|  - include discovered skills list in prompt
126pg|- otherwise:
127de|  - omit discovered list
128th|
129ve|Task tool subagents receive the session's discovered/provided skills list via normal session creation; there is no per-task skill pinning override.
130th|
131qx|### Interactive `/skill:<name>` commands
132nd|
133np|If `skills.enableSkillCommands` is true, interactive mode registers one slash command per discovered skill.
134th|
135au|`/skill:<name> [args]` behavior:
136th|
137pz|- reads the skill file directly from `filePath`
138jq|- strips frontmatter
139sf|- injects skill body as a follow-up custom message
140fh|- appends metadata (`Skill: <path>`, optional `User: <args>`)
141st|
142pr|## `skill://` URL behavior
143rd|
144pj|`src/internal-urls/skill-protocol.ts` supports:
145th|
146rb|- `skill://<name>` → resolves to that skill's `SKILL.md`
147li|- `skill://<name>/<relative-path>` → resolves inside that skill directory
148th|
149xc|```text
150fd|skill:// URL resolution
151st|
152sm|skill://pdf
153yd|  -> <pdf-base>/SKILL.md
154th|
155sr|skill://pdf/references/tables.md
156pq|  -> <pdf-base>/references/tables.md
157th|
158cn|Guards:
159mz|- reject absolute paths
160rp|- reject `..` traversal
161fp|- reject any resolved path escaping <pdf-base>
162tu|```
163rd|
164da|Resolution details:
165th|
166qh|- skill name must match exactly
167ss|- relative paths are URL-decoded
168cw|- absolute paths are rejected
169lg|- path traversal (`..`) is rejected
170nw|- resolved path must remain within `baseDir`
171vw|- missing files return an explicit `File not found` error
172nd|
173mk|Content type:
174th|
175mi|- `.md` => `text/markdown`
176ln|- everything else => `text/plain`
177th|
178ve|No fallback search is performed for missing assets.
179th|
180tu|## Skills vs AGENTS.md, commands, tools, hooks
181st|
182ae|### Skills vs AGENTS.md
183rd|
184ce|- **Skills**: named, optional capability packs selected by task context or explicitly requested
185rx|- **AGENTS.md/context files**: persistent instruction files loaded as context-file capability and merged by level/depth rules
186th|
187hy|`src/discovery/agents-md.ts` specifically walks ancestor directories from `cwd` to discover standalone `AGENTS.md` files (up to depth 20), excluding hidden-directory segments.
188th|
189hx|### Skills vs slash commands
190th|
191cc|- **Skills**: model-readable knowledge/workflow content
192jq|- **Slash commands**: user-invoked command entry points
193eq|- `/skill:<name>` is a convenience wrapper that injects skill text; it does not change skill discovery semantics
194th|
195wk|### Skills vs custom tools
196th|
197zd|- **Skills**: documentation/workflow content loaded through prompt context and `read`
198qy|- **Custom tools**: executable tool APIs callable by the model with schemas and runtime side effects
199th|
200da|### Skills vs hooks
201st|
202tv|- **Skills**: passive content
203ji|- **Hooks**: event-driven runtime interceptors that can block/modify behavior during execution
204th|
205zy|## Practical authoring guidance tied to discovery logic
206th|
207jx|- Put each skill in its own directory: `<skills-root>/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`
208ej|- Always include explicit `name` and `description` frontmatter
209wj|- Keep referenced assets under the same skill directory and access with `skill://<name>/...`
210ny|- For nested taxonomy (`team/domain/skill`), point `skills.customDirectories` to the nested parent directory; scanning itself remains non-recursive
211jr|- Avoid duplicate skill names across sources; first match wins by provider precedence
212th|

2ce|name: authoring-extensions
3bk|description: Use when creating a new omp extension. Covers ExtensionAPI, factory signature, tool/command/event registration, and local-dev testing.
4ue|---
5th|
6sz|# Authoring Extensions
7th|
8na|Extensions are the primary way to add capabilities to `oh-my-pi`. A single extension module can register tools the LLM can call, slash commands users can invoke, and event handlers that run throughout the session lifecycle — all from one TypeScript file.
9th|
10lz|## Minimum viable extension
11th|
12mq|```ts
13ma|import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
14th|
15nz|export default function (pi: ExtensionAPI) {
16hj|  pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
17sf|    ctx.ui.notify("My extension loaded!", "info");
18ds|  });
19th|}
20wo|```
21st|
22vr|That is a working extension. Drop it into `~/.omp/agent/extensions/hello.ts` and restart omp to see the notification.
23rd|
24it|## Full example
25th|
26ba|The following extension registers a slash command, a tool, and a session-start hook:
27th|
28mq|```ts
29ma|import type { ExtensionAPI } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
30th|
31xa|export default function myExtension(pi: ExtensionAPI) {
32bu|  const { Type } = pi.typebox;
33rd|
34ih|  // Runs once when the session loads
35hj|  pi.on("session_start", async (_event, ctx) => {
36fy|    ctx.ui.notify(`Session ready in ${ctx.cwd}`, "info");
37vi|  });
38th|
39vy|  // Slash command: /greet
40eb|  pi.registerCommand("greet", {
41tp|    description: "Send a greeting into the conversation",
42yv|    handler: async (args, ctx) => {
43lj|      const name = args.trim() || "world";
44hz|      pi.sendMessage(
45th|        {
46ue|          customType: "greeting",
47ng|          content: `Hello, ${name}!`,
48uw|          display: true,
49er|          attribution: "user",
50et|        },
51uy|        { triggerTurn: false }
52rk|      );
53np|      ctx.ui.notify(`Greeted ${name}`, "info");
54mn|    },
55ay|  });
56th|
57rw|  // LLM-callable tool
58uy|  pi.registerTool({
59lc|    name: "word_count",
60od|    label: "Word Count",
61yk|    description: "Count the words in a string",
62zb|    parameters: Type.Object({
63mz|      text: Type.String({ description: "Text to count" }),
64mk|    }),
65gc|    async execute(_id, params, _signal, _onUpdate, _ctx) {
66ki|      const count = params.text.split(/\s+/).filter(Boolean).length;
67ou|      return {
68yl|        content: [{ type: "text", text: String(count) }],
69uz|        details: { count },
70ya|      };
71jf|    },
72uc|  });
73rd|}
74ux|```
75th|
76fd|## Discovery path
77th|
78wo|omp discovers extension modules in this order:
79th|
80vm|1. **Project-scoped auto-discovery** — `<cwd>/.omp/extensions/`
81ho|2. **User-scoped auto-discovery** — `~/.omp/agent/extensions/`
82yg|3. **Marketplace-installed plugins** — `~/.omp/plugins/node_modules/` (extensions shipped inside installed plugin packages)
83lv|4. **CLI flag** — `omp --extension ./my-ext.ts` (also `-e`; `--hook` is treated as an alias)
84st|5. **Settings `extensions` array** — paths listed in `~/.omp/agent/config.yml` or `<cwd>/.omp/settings.json`
85th|
86om|Within each scope, de-duplication is by resolved absolute path — first seen wins.
87th|
88fx|When a path points to a directory, omp resolves the entry point in this order:
89th|
90mb|1. `package.json` with `omp.extensions` (or legacy `pi.extensions`) field
91vs|2. `index.ts`
92oa|3. `index.js`
93an|4. One-level scan for `*.ts` / `*.js` files and subdir `index.*` / `package.json` manifests
94th|
95es|## package.json manifest
96th|
97uz|To package an extension as an installable plugin, add an `omp` field to `package.json`:
98th|
99cp|```json
100th|{
101kl|  "name": "my-omp-extension",
102pr|  "omp": {
103st|    "extensions": ["./src/main.ts"]
104th|  }
105th|}
106pv|```
107th|
108do|The legacy `pi` key is also accepted for backwards compatibility:
109th|
110cp|```json
111th|{
112jc|  "pi": {
113ui|    "extensions": ["./index.ts"]
114th|  }
115th|}
116wd|```
117th|
118oe|Multiple entry points are supported:
119th|
120cp|```json
121st|{
122pr|  "omp": {
123ml|    "extensions": ["./src/safety.ts", "./src/tools.ts"]
124th|  }
125th|}
126jc|```
127th|
128qc|## Registering commands
129th|
130mq|```ts
131ub|pi.registerCommand("my-cmd", {
132um|  description: "What the command does",
133nm|  handler: async (args, ctx) => {
134sk|    // args: everything the user typed after /my-cmd
135li|    // ctx: ExtensionCommandContext — includes ctx.ui, ctx.cwd, session controls
136ii|    ctx.ui.notify("Running!", "info");
137mz|    await ctx.waitForIdle();
138vb|    await ctx.newSession();
139dg|  },
140bl|});
141uq|```
142nd|
143at|`ExtensionCommandContext` session-control methods (safe to call from commands only):
144th|
145ua|| Method | Effect |
146mb||---|---|
147hn|| `waitForIdle()` | Wait for the agent to finish streaming |
148cf|| `newSession(opts?)` | Open a fresh session |
149yb|| `switchSession(path)` | Switch to an existing session file |
150uv|| `branch(entryId)` | Fork from a specific history entry |
151sv|| `navigateTree(id, opts?)` | Jump to a different point in the session tree |
152xl|| `reload()` | Reload the session runtime |
153sm|| `compact(opts?)` | Compact the current context |
154th|
155wd|## Registering tools
156th|
157rc|Tools are called by the LLM. Parameters use [TypeBox](https://github.com/sinclairzx81/typebox) schemas, available at `pi.typebox`:
158th|
159mq|```ts
160ae|const { Type } = pi.typebox;
161st|
162df|pi.registerTool({
163gr|  name: "search_notes",           // snake_case, unique
164df|  label: "Search Notes",          // human-readable label for TUI
165nc|  description: "Full-text search through project notes",
166ra|  parameters: Type.Object({
167tm|    query: Type.String({ description: "Search query" }),
168jj|    limit: Type.Optional(Type.Number({ description: "Max results", default: 10 })),
169gr|  }),
170hh|  async execute(toolCallId, params, signal, onUpdate, ctx) {
171bu|    if (signal?.aborted) {
172os|      return { content: [{ type: "text", text: "Cancelled" }] };
173rd|    }
174mz|    onUpdate?.({ content: [{ type: "text", text: "Searching..." }] });
175qr|    // ... do work ...
176ga|    return {
177uz|      content: [{ type: "text", text: `Found N results for "${params.query}"` }],
178eh|      details: { query: params.query, count: 0 },
179zx|    };
180za|  },
181wd|});
182gu|```
183rd|
184jd|## Subscribing to events
185th|
186mq|```ts
187hz|pi.on("tool_call", async (event, ctx) => {
188gz|  // event.toolName, event.input, event.toolCallId
189ni|  if (event.toolName !== "bash") return;
190th|
191lg|  const command = String((event.input as { command?: unknown }).command ?? "");
192hj|  if (command.includes("rm -rf /")) {
193lc|    return { block: true, reason: "Blocked by safety policy" };
194th|  }
195xc|});
196th|
197am|pi.on("turn_end", async (_event, ctx) => {
198dz|  ctx.ui.setStatus("tokens", `~${ctx.getContextUsage()?.tokens ?? "?"} tokens`);
199bn|});
200yc|```
201st|
202ad|Full event catalog: see [hooks authoring guide](./authoring-hooks.md).
203rd|
204ar|## Extension vs hook — when to use which
205th|
206kj|| Need | Use |
207os||---|---|
208ef|| Tools + commands + events in one module | **Extension** (`ExtensionAPI`) |
209lg|| Pure event interception (policy, redaction) | **Extension** or **Hook** (both work; extension is preferred) |
210nf|| Legacy hook module already exists | **Hook** (`HookAPI` from `@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent/extensibility/hooks`) |
211qn|| Registering provider / custom message renderer | **Extension only** |
212tz|| Shipping as a marketplace plugin | **Extension** (use `package.json` manifest) |
213th|
214qu|Extensions are a strict superset of hooks. New authoring should use `ExtensionAPI`.
215th|
216lf|## Debugging
217th|
218ih|Start omp with `--log-level debug` to see extension load messages:
219th|
220er|```
221oj|omp --log-level debug
222go|```
223rd|
224fw|Watch for lines like:
225th|
226vs|```
227hk|[extension-loader] loading /home/you/.omp/agent/extensions/my-ext.ts
228bt|[extension-loader] loaded: my-ext (1 tool, 1 command, 2 handlers)
229qy|```
230th|
231zt|To temporarily disable a specific extension by name without removing the file:
232nd|
233ye|```yaml
234yz|# ~/.omp/agent/config.yml
235zy|disabledExtensions:
236ls|  - extension-module:my-ext
237eq|```
238th|
239up|The derived name is the filename stem (or directory name for `index.ts`-style entries): `/path/to/my-ext.ts` → `my-ext`.
240th|
241ti|## Important constraints
242nd|
243ke|- **Do not call runtime actions during load.** Methods like `pi.sendMessage()` throw `ExtensionRuntimeNotInitializedError` if called synchronously during module evaluation (before a session is active). Register handlers/tools/commands during load; perform runtime actions only from event handlers, tools, or commands.
244aj|- **`tool_call` errors are fail-closed.** If a `tool_call` handler throws, the tool is blocked.
245jd|- **Command names must not clash with built-ins.** Conflicts are skipped with a diagnostic log.
246on|- **Reserved shortcuts are ignored** (`ctrl+c`, `ctrl+d`, `ctrl+z`, `ctrl+k`, `ctrl+p`, `ctrl+l`, `ctrl+o`, `ctrl+t`, `ctrl+g`, `shift+tab`, `shift+ctrl+p`, `alt+enter`, `escape`, `enter`).
247th|
248ax|## Further reading
249th|
250pc|- `docs/extensions.md` — runtime internals and full API surface reference
251ib|- `docs/extension-loading.md` — detailed path resolution rules
252ez|- `docs/hooks.md` — hook subsystem internals
253fk|- `docs/skills/examples/hello-extension/` — complete working example
254th|

1fu|# Task Agent Discovery and Selection
2nd|
3ox|This document describes how the task subsystem discovers agent definitions, merges multiple sources, and resolves a requested agent at execution time.
4th|
5dl|It covers runtime behavior as implemented today, including precedence, invalid-definition handling, and spawn/depth constraints that can make an agent effectively unavailable.
6th|
7lo|## Implementation files
8th|
9rn|- [`src/task/discovery.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/discovery.ts)
10jr|- [`src/task/agents.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/agents.ts)
11kx|- [`src/task/types.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/types.ts)
12fd|- [`src/task/index.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/index.ts)
13hb|- [`src/task/commands.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/commands.ts)
14fr|- [`src/prompts/agents/task.md`](../packages/coding-agent/src/prompts/agents/task.md)
15ws|- [`src/prompts/tools/task.md`](../packages/coding-agent/src/prompts/tools/task.md)
16do|- [`src/discovery/helpers.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/discovery/helpers.ts)
17bg|- [`src/config.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/config.ts)
18pe|- [`src/task/executor.ts`](../packages/coding-agent/src/task/executor.ts)
19th|
20jq|---
21st|
22hz|## Agent definition shape
23rd|
24st|Task agents normalize into `AgentDefinition` (`src/task/types.ts`):
25th|
26ui|- `name`, `description`, `systemPrompt` (required for a valid loaded agent)
27pt|- optional `tools`, `spawns`, `model`, `thinkingLevel`, `output`, `blocking`
28su|- `source`: `"bundled" | "user" | "project"`
29rq|- optional `filePath`
30th|
31ot|Parsing comes from frontmatter via `parseAgentFields()` (`src/discovery/helpers.ts`):
32nd|
33ct|- missing `name` or `description` => invalid (`null`), caller treats as parse failure
34ie|- `tools` accepts CSV or array; if provided, `yield` is auto-added
35ay|- `spawns` accepts `*`, CSV, or array
36rc|- backward-compat behavior: if `spawns` missing but `tools` includes `task`, `spawns` becomes `*`
37aa|- `output` is passed through as opaque schema data
38th|
39an|## Bundled agents
40th|
41ou|Bundled agents are embedded at build time (`src/task/agents.ts`) using text imports.
42nd|
43jj|`EMBEDDED_AGENT_DEFS` defines:
44th|
45qm|- `explore`, `plan`, `designer`, `reviewer` from prompt files
46nl|- `task` and `quick_task` from shared `task.md` body plus injected frontmatter
47th|
48ru|Loading path:
49th|
50sb|1. `loadBundledAgents()` parses embedded markdown with `parseAgent(..., "bundled", "fatal")`
51gr|2. results are cached in-memory (`bundledAgentsCache`)
52mg|3. `clearBundledAgentsCache()` is test-only cache reset
53rd|
54ru|Because bundled parsing uses `level: "fatal"`, malformed bundled frontmatter throws and can fail discovery entirely.
55th|
56kk|## Filesystem and plugin discovery
57th|
58xs|`discoverAgents(cwd, home)` (`src/task/discovery.ts`) merges agents from multiple places before appending bundled definitions.
59th|
60rd|### Discovery inputs
61st|
62fr|1. User config agent dirs from `getConfigDirs("agents", { project: false })`
63zp|2. Nearest project agent dirs from `findAllNearestProjectConfigDirs("agents", cwd)`
64yt|3. Claude plugin roots (`listClaudePluginRoots(home)`) with `agents/` subdirs
65te|4. Bundled agents (`loadBundledAgents()`)
66th|
67yi|### Actual source order
68th|
69et|Source-family order comes from `getConfigDirs("", { project: false })`, which is derived from `priorityList` in `src/config.ts`:
70th|
71ty|1. `.omp`
72ae|2. `.claude`
73ia|3. `.codex`
74un|4. `.gemini`
75th|
76vu|For each source family, discovery order is:
77th|
78ph|1. nearest project dir for that source (if found)
79an|2. user dir for that source
80th|
81jw|After all source-family dirs, plugin `agents/` dirs are appended (project-scope plugins first, then user-scope).
82nd|
83tb|Bundled agents are appended last.
84th|
85bi|### Important caveat: stale comments vs current code
86th|
87bl|`discovery.ts` header comments still mention `.pi` and do not mention `.codex`/`.gemini`. Actual runtime order is driven by `src/config.ts` and currently uses `.omp`, `.claude`, `.codex`, `.gemini`.
88th|
89ch|## Merge and collision rules
90th|
91fe|Discovery uses first-wins dedup by exact `agent.name`:
92nd|
93im|- A `Set<string>` tracks seen names.
94ao|- Loaded agents are flattened in directory order and kept only if name unseen.
95ek|- Bundled agents are filtered against the same set and only added if still unseen.
96th|
97cq|Implications:
98th|
99in|- Project overrides user for same source family.
100sl|- Higher-priority source family overrides lower (`.omp` before `.claude`, etc.).
101hb|- Non-bundled agents override bundled agents with the same name.
102il|- Name matching is case-sensitive (`Task` and `task` are distinct).
103ej|- Within one directory, markdown files are read in lexicographic filename order before dedup.
104th|
105xo|## Invalid/missing agent file behavior
106th|
107oh|Per directory (`loadAgentsFromDir`):
108th|
109gd|- unreadable/missing directory: treated as empty (`readdir(...).catch(() => [])`)
110un|- file read or parse failure: warning logged, file skipped
111rv|- parse path uses `parseAgent(..., level: "warn")`
112th|
113lo|Frontmatter failure behavior comes from `parseFrontmatter`:
114th|
115af|- parse error at `warn` level logs warning
116rg|- parser falls back to a simple `key: value` line parser
117vf|- if required fields are still missing, `parseAgentFields` fails, then `AgentParsingError` is thrown and caught by caller (file skipped)
118th|
119im|Net effect: one bad custom agent file does not abort discovery of other files.
120th|
121op|## Agent lookup and selection
122nd|
123av|Lookup is exact-name linear search:
124th|
125zi|- `getAgent(agents, name)` => `agents.find(a => a.name === name)`
126th|
127lz|In synchronous task execution (`TaskTool.#executeSync`):
128th|
129jo|1. agents are rediscovered at execution time (`discoverAgents(this.session.cwd)`)
130af|2. requested `params.agent` is resolved through `getAgent`
131qx|3. missing agent returns immediate tool response:
132lb|   - `Unknown agent "...". Available: ...`
133nb|   - no subprocess runs
134th|
135jo|### Description vs execution-time discovery
136th|
137ey|`TaskTool.create()` builds the tool description from discovery results at initialization time. `#executeSync` rediscovers agents, so the runtime set can differ from what was listed in the earlier tool description if agent files changed mid-session. The async entry path still uses the initialization-time list to decide whether an agent is marked `blocking` before scheduling.
138th|
139tz|## Structured-output guardrails and schema precedence
140th|
141vx|Runtime output schema precedence in `TaskTool.execute`:
142nd|
143fy|1. task call `params.schema` when `task.simple` allows custom schemas
144bc|2. agent frontmatter `output`
145pl|3. parent session `outputSchema`
146th|
147pu|(`effectiveOutputSchema = outputSchema ?? effectiveAgent.output ?? this.session.outputSchema` when custom task schemas are enabled; otherwise task-call schema is skipped.)
148th|
149ky|Prompt-time guardrail text in `src/prompts/tools/task.md` warns about mismatch behavior for structured-output agents (`explore`, `reviewer`): output-format instructions in prose can conflict with built-in schema and produce `null` outputs.
150th|
151ex|This is guidance, not hard runtime validation logic in `discoverAgents`.
152nd|
153rh|## Command discovery interaction
154th|
155gi|`src/task/commands.ts` is parallel infrastructure for workflow commands (not agent definitions), but it follows the same overall pattern:
156th|
157jo|- discover from capability providers first
158xl|- deduplicate by name with first-wins
159yt|- append bundled commands if still unseen
160fn|- exact-name lookup via `getCommand`
161st|
162el|In `src/task/index.ts`, command helpers are re-exported with agent discovery helpers. Agent discovery itself does not depend on command discovery at runtime.
163rd|
164pm|## Availability constraints beyond discovery
165th|
166lb|An agent can be discoverable but still unavailable to run because of execution guardrails.
167th|
168rp|### Disabled-agent settings
169th|
170sw|`TaskTool.#executeSync` checks `task.disabledAgents` after resolving the agent. If the requested name is disabled, execution returns an immediate error listing enabled alternatives when available.
171st|
172sx|### Parent spawn policy
173rd|
174yu|`TaskTool.#executeSync` checks `session.getSessionSpawns()`:
175th|
176pr|- `"*"` => allow any
177za|- `""` => deny all
178mx|- CSV list => allow only listed names
179th|
180pu|If denied: immediate `Cannot spawn '...'. Allowed: ...` response.
181st|
182ye|### Blocked self-recursion env guard
183rd|
184at|`PI_BLOCKED_AGENT` is read at tool construction. If request matches, execution is rejected with recursion-prevention message.
185th|
186hf|### Recursion-depth gating (task tool availability inside child sessions)
187th|
188ou|In `runSubprocess` (`src/task/executor.ts`):
189th|
190va|- depth computed from `taskDepth`
191ok|- `task.maxRecursionDepth` controls cutoff
192rq|- when at max depth:
193ub|  - `task` tool is removed from child tool list
194an|  - child `spawns` env is set to empty
195th|
196ob|So deeper levels cannot spawn further tasks even if the agent definition includes `spawns`.
197th|
198lz|## Plan mode caveat (current implementation)
199th|
200kd|`TaskTool.execute` computes an `effectiveAgent` for plan mode (prepends plan-mode prompt, forces read-only tool subset, clears spawns), but `runSubprocess` is called with `agent` rather than `effectiveAgent`.
201st|
202ks|Current effect:
203rd|
204ua|- model override / thinking level / output schema are derived from `effectiveAgent`
205tm|- system prompt and tool/spawn restrictions from `effectiveAgent` are not passed through in this call path
206th|
207fz|This is an implementation caveat worth knowing when reading plan-mode behavior expectations.
208th|

1nz|# SDK
2nd|
3dm|The SDK is the in-process integration surface for `@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent`.
4af|Use it when you want direct access to agent state, event streaming, tool wiring, and session control from your own Bun/Node process.
5th|
6ud|If you need cross-language/process isolation, use RPC mode instead.
7th|
8vc|## Installation
9th|
10pc|```bash
11cn|bun add @oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent
12vi|```
13th|
14uk|## Entry points
15th|
16bo|`@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent` exports the SDK APIs from the package root (and also via `@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent/sdk`).
17th|
18av|Core exports for embedders:
19th|
20zd|- `createAgentSession`
21yy|- `SessionManager`
22lu|- `Settings`
23uj|- `AuthStorage`
24se|- `ModelRegistry`
25js|- `discoverAuthStorage`
26om|- Discovery helpers (`discoverExtensions`, `discoverSkills`, `discoverContextFiles`, `discoverPromptTemplates`, `discoverSlashCommands`, `discoverCustomTSCommands`, `discoverMCPServers`)
27qo|- Tool factory surface (`createTools`, `BUILTIN_TOOLS`, tool classes)
28th|
29ub|## Quick start (auto-discovery defaults)
30th|
31mq|```ts
32he|import { createAgentSession } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
33rd|
34sq|const { session, modelFallbackMessage } = await createAgentSession();
35th|
36jy|if (modelFallbackMessage) {
37rg|  process.stderr.write(`${modelFallbackMessage}\n`);
38th|}
39th|
40pv|const unsubscribe = session.subscribe((event) => {
41xo|  if (
42ax|    event.type === "message_update" &&
43bm|    event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta"
44js|  ) {
45hf|    process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
46th|  }
47fn|});
48th|
49hp|await session.prompt("Summarize this repository in 3 bullets.");
50bi|unsubscribe();
51jn|await session.dispose();
52tk|```
53rd|
54qt|## What `createAgentSession()` discovers by default
55th|
56mq|`createAgentSession()` follows “provide to override, omit to discover”.
57th|
58bf|If omitted, it resolves:
59th|
60hp|- `cwd`: `getProjectDir()`
61hf|- `agentDir`: `~/.omp/agent` (via `getAgentDir()`)
62zo|- `authStorage`: `discoverAuthStorage(agentDir)`
63tj|- `modelRegistry`: `new ModelRegistry(authStorage)` + background `refreshInBackground()` when the registry is not provided
64pq|- `settings`: `await Settings.init({ cwd, agentDir })`
65yw|- `sessionManager`: `SessionManager.create(cwd)` (file-backed)
66il|- skills/context files/prompt templates/slash commands/extensions/custom TS commands
67di|- built-in tools via `createTools(...)`
68mb|- MCP tools (enabled by default)
69ga|- LSP integration (enabled by default)
70ll|- `eventBus`: new `EventBus()` unless supplied
71st|
72ii|### Required vs optional inputs
73rd|
74iw|Typically you must provide only what you want to control:
75th|
76vb|- **Must provide**: nothing for a minimal session
77dn|- **Usually provide explicitly** in embedders:
78kf|  - `sessionManager` (if you need in-memory or custom location)
79ep|  - `authStorage` + `modelRegistry` (if you own credential/model lifecycle)
80cj|  - `model` or `modelPattern` (if deterministic model selection matters)
81bp|  - `settings` (if you need isolated/test config)
82nd|
83ty|## Session manager behavior (persistent vs in-memory)
84th|
85ge|`AgentSession` always uses a `SessionManager`; behavior depends on which factory you use.
86th|
87pn|### File-backed (default)
88th|
89mq|```ts
90qo|import { createAgentSession, SessionManager } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
91st|
92pw|const { session } = await createAgentSession({
93sa|  sessionManager: SessionManager.create(process.cwd()),
94va|});
95th|
96kp|console.log(session.sessionFile); // absolute .jsonl path
97jp|```
98th|
99yw|- Persists conversation/messages/state deltas to session files.
100ho|- Supports resume/open/list/fork workflows.
101tv|- `session.sessionFile` is defined.
102nd|
103jl|### In-memory
104th|
105mq|```ts
106qo|import { createAgentSession, SessionManager } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
107th|
108pw|const { session } = await createAgentSession({
109uy|  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
110ya|});
111th|
112ux|console.log(session.sessionFile); // undefined
113nd|```
114th|
115ec|- No filesystem persistence.
116ch|- Useful for tests, ephemeral workers, request-scoped agents.
117td|- Session methods still work, but persistence-specific behaviors (file resume/fork paths) are naturally limited.
118th|
119ib|### Resume/open/list helpers
120th|
121mq|```ts
122ip|import { SessionManager } from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
123rd|
124hq|const recent = await SessionManager.continueRecent(process.cwd());
125nn|const listed = await SessionManager.list(process.cwd());
126tw|const opened = listed[0] ? await SessionManager.open(listed[0].path) : null;
127be|```
128th|
129ac|## Model and auth wiring
130th|
131uy|`createAgentSession()` uses `ModelRegistry` + `AuthStorage` for model selection and API key resolution.
132nd|
133hn|### Explicit wiring
134th|
135mq|```ts
136fa|import {
137ex|  createAgentSession,
138qs|  discoverAuthStorage,
139ql|  ModelRegistry,
140oz|  SessionManager,
141ti|} from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
142nd|
143aj|const authStorage = await discoverAuthStorage();
144yj|const modelRegistry = new ModelRegistry(authStorage);
145st|await modelRegistry.refresh();
146th|
147ot|const available = modelRegistry.getAvailable();
148uj|if (available.length === 0)
149nb|  throw new Error("No authenticated models available");
150th|
151pw|const { session } = await createAgentSession({
152xi|  authStorage,
153ds|  modelRegistry,
154dd|  model: available[0],
155mh|  thinkingLevel: "medium",
156uy|  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
157jk|});
158gj|```
159th|
160es|### Selection order when `model` is omitted
161st|
162an|When no explicit `model`/`modelPattern` is provided:
163rd|
164rp|1. restore model from existing session (if restorable + key available)
165ey|2. settings default model role (`default`)
166hv|3. first available model with valid auth
167th|
168wt|If restore fails, `modelFallbackMessage` explains fallback.
169th|
170mu|### Auth priority
171st|
172vy|`AuthStorage.getApiKey(...)` resolves in this order:
173rd|
174ko|1. runtime override (`setRuntimeApiKey`)
175lx|2. stored credentials in `agent.db`
176sr|3. provider environment variables
177yc|4. custom-provider resolver fallback (if configured)
178th|
179sq|## Event subscription model
180th|
181yb|Subscribe with `session.subscribe(listener)`; it returns an unsubscribe function.
182nd|
183mq|```ts
184pv|const unsubscribe = session.subscribe((event) => {
185ub|  switch (event.type) {
186of|    case "agent_start":
187rr|    case "turn_start":
188aj|    case "tool_execution_start":
189ak|      break;
190eq|    case "message_update":
191qu|      if (event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta") {
192gj|        process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
193rd|      }
194ak|      break;
195th|  }
196mm|});
197jb|```
198th|
199jw|`AgentSessionEvent` includes core `AgentEvent` plus session-level events:
200th|
201sh|- `auto_compaction_start` / `auto_compaction_end`
202ud|- `auto_retry_start` / `auto_retry_end`
203sy|- `retry_fallback_applied` / `retry_fallback_succeeded`
204ae|- `ttsr_triggered`
205qa|- `todo_reminder` / `todo_auto_clear`
206nj|- `irc_message`
207th|
208am|## Prompt lifecycle
209th|
210jd|`session.prompt(text, options?)` is the primary entry point.
211th|
212qu|Behavior:
213th|
214vx|1. optional command/template expansion (`/` commands, custom commands, file slash commands, prompt templates)
215xy|2. if currently streaming:
216ci|   - requires `streamingBehavior: "steer" | "followUp"`
217jy|   - queues instead of throwing work away
218qw|3. if idle:
219za|   - validates model + API key
220wc|   - appends user message
221ln|   - starts agent turn
222nd|
223eg|Related APIs:
224th|
225md|- `sendUserMessage(content, { deliverAs? })`
226uc|- `steer(text, images?)`
227xp|- `followUp(text, images?)`
228uf|- `sendCustomMessage({ customType, content, ... }, { deliverAs?, triggerTurn? })`
229oh|- `abort()`
230th|
231oh|## Tools and extension integration
232nd|
233qb|### Built-ins and filtering
234th|
235tz|- Built-ins come from `createTools(...)` and `BUILTIN_TOOLS`.
236pm|- `toolNames` acts as an allowlist for built-ins.
237fl|- `customTools` and extension-registered tools are still included.
238gz|- Hidden tools (for example `yield`) are opt-in unless required by options.
239th|
240mq|```ts
241pw|const { session } = await createAgentSession({
242iq|  toolNames: ["read", "grep", "find", "write"],
243jn|  requireYieldTool: true,
244ys|});
245ui|```
246th|
247hi|### Extensions
248th|
249jr|- `extensions`: inline `ExtensionFactory[]`
250wl|- `additionalExtensionPaths`: load extra extension files
251zm|- `disableExtensionDiscovery`: disable automatic extension scanning
252xh|- `preloadedExtensions`: reuse already loaded extension set
253rd|
254kv|### Runtime tool set changes
255th|
256bx|`AgentSession` supports runtime activation updates:
257th|
258oq|- `getActiveToolNames()`
259gz|- `getAllToolNames()`
260ns|- `setActiveToolsByName(names)`
261qc|- `refreshMCPTools(mcpTools)`
262nd|
263nz|System prompt is rebuilt to reflect active tool changes.
264th|
265vq|## Discovery helpers
266th|
267ur|Use these when you want partial control without recreating internal discovery logic:
268th|
269eh|- `discoverAuthStorage(agentDir?)`
270lm|- `discoverExtensions(cwd?)`
271nz|- `discoverSkills(cwd?, _agentDir?, settings?)`
272bu|- `discoverContextFiles(cwd?, _agentDir?)`
273zx|- `discoverPromptTemplates(cwd?, agentDir?)`
274dq|- `discoverSlashCommands(cwd?)`
275mv|- `discoverCustomTSCommands(cwd?, agentDir?)`
276ck|- `discoverMCPServers(cwd?)`
277bc|- `buildSystemPrompt(options?)`
278th|
279hl|## Subagent-oriented options
280th|
281jx|For SDK consumers building orchestrators (similar to task executor flow):
282nd|
283uh|- `outputSchema`: passes structured output expectation into tool context
284tp|- `requireYieldTool`: forces `yield` tool inclusion
285tk|- `taskDepth`: recursion-depth context for nested task sessions
286yo|- `parentTaskPrefix`: artifact naming prefix for nested task outputs
287th|
288ps|These are optional for normal single-agent embedding.
289th|
290yz|## `createAgentSession()` return value
291st|
292mq|```ts
293qq|type CreateAgentSessionResult = {
294cv|  session: AgentSession;
295ry|  extensionsResult: LoadExtensionsResult;
296vr|  setToolUIContext: (uiContext: ExtensionUIContext, hasUI: boolean) => void;
297dd|  mcpManager?: MCPManager;
298ln|  modelFallbackMessage?: string;
299uc|  lspServers?: Array<{
300eo|    name: string;
301sj|    status: "ready" | "error";
302rx|    fileTypes: string[];
303hh|    error?: string;
304st|  }>;
305iv|  eventBus: EventBus;
306st|};
307gs|```
308th|
309qh|Use `setToolUIContext(...)` only if your embedder provides UI capabilities that tools/extensions should call into.
310th|
311lo|## Minimal controlled embed example
312th|
313mq|```ts
314fa|import {
315ex|  createAgentSession,
316qs|  discoverAuthStorage,
317ql|  ModelRegistry,
318oz|  SessionManager,
319mu|  Settings,
320ti|} from "@oh-my-pi/pi-coding-agent";
321st|
322aj|const authStorage = await discoverAuthStorage();
323yj|const modelRegistry = new ModelRegistry(authStorage);
324st|await modelRegistry.refresh();
325th|
326em|const settings = Settings.isolated({
327sz|  "compaction.enabled": true,
328qy|  "retry.enabled": true,
329ro|});
330th|
331pw|const { session } = await createAgentSession({
332xi|  authStorage,
333ds|  modelRegistry,
334nj|  settings,
335uy|  sessionManager: SessionManager.inMemory(),
336ph|  toolNames: ["read", "grep", "find", "edit", "write"],
337fv|  enableMCP: false,
338as|  enableLsp: true,
339hr|});
340th|
341jj|session.subscribe((event) => {
342xo|  if (
343ax|    event.type === "message_update" &&
344bm|    event.assistantMessageEvent.type === "text_delta"
345mn|  ) {
346hf|    process.stdout.write(event.assistantMessageEvent.delta);
347th|  }
348iu|});
349th|
350sd|await session.prompt("Find all TODO comments in this repo and propose fixes.");
351jn|await session.dispose();
352vi|```

1ad|# Configuration Discovery and Resolution
2nd|
3sb|This document describes how the coding-agent resolves configuration today: which roots are scanned, how precedence works, and how resolved config is consumed by settings, skills, hooks, tools, and extensions.
4th|
5ci|## Scope
6th|
7ok|Primary implementation:
8th|
9fi|- `packages/coding-agent/src/config.ts`
10nf|- `packages/coding-agent/src/config/settings.ts`
11ih|- `packages/coding-agent/src/config/settings-schema.ts`
12hk|- `packages/coding-agent/src/discovery/builtin.ts`
13hi|- `packages/coding-agent/src/discovery/helpers.ts`
14th|
15af|Key integration points:
16th|
17wu|- `packages/coding-agent/src/capability/index.ts`
18av|- `packages/coding-agent/src/discovery/index.ts`
19oq|- `packages/coding-agent/src/extensibility/skills.ts`
20cn|- `packages/coding-agent/src/extensibility/hooks/loader.ts`
21vw|- `packages/coding-agent/src/extensibility/custom-tools/loader.ts`
22mu|- `packages/coding-agent/src/extensibility/extensions/loader.ts`
23rd|
24tn|---
25th|
26wm|## Resolution flow (visual)
27th|
28xc|```text
29jo|         Generic helper order (`config.ts`)
30xb|┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
31fm|│ 1) ~/.omp/agent, ~/.claude, ...       │
32ax|│ 2) <cwd>/.omp, <cwd>/.claude, ...     │
33gl|└───────────────────────────────────────┘
34tk|                    │
35fn|                    ▼
36qm|        capability providers enumerate items
37lu| (native provider scans project .omp before user .omp;
38he|  other providers have their own loading rules)
39pt|                    │
40eg|                    ▼
41cv|      provider priority sort + capability dedup
42aj|                    │
43on|                    ▼
44tv|          subsystem-specific consumption
45tp|   (settings, skills, hooks, tools, extensions)
46zp|```
47th|
48ro|## 1) Config roots and source order
49th|
50fl|## Canonical roots
51st|
52hn|`src/config.ts` defines a fixed source priority list:
53rd|
54fv|1. `.omp` (native)
55ae|2. `.claude`
56ia|3. `.codex`
57un|4. `.gemini`
58th|
59dw|User-level bases:
60th|
61yb|- `~/.omp/agent`
62yo|- `~/.claude`
63ka|- `~/.codex`
64xs|- `~/.gemini`
65th|
66ru|Project-level bases:
67th|
68nv|- `<cwd>/.omp`
69cs|- `<cwd>/.claude`
70yf|- `<cwd>/.codex`
71vu|- `<cwd>/.gemini`
72nd|
73ps|`CONFIG_DIR_NAME` is `.omp` (`packages/utils/src/dirs.ts`).
74th|
75nz|## Important constraint
76th|
77zr|The generic helpers in `src/config.ts` do **not** include `.pi` in source discovery order.
78th|
79cp|---
80th|
81oj|## 2) Core discovery helpers (`src/config.ts`)
82nd|
83xt|## `getConfigDirs(subpath, options)`
84th|
85ky|Returns ordered entries:
86th|
87df|- User-level entries first (by source priority)
88kp|- Then project-level entries (by same source priority)
89th|
90nf|Options:
91st|
92ea|- `user` (default `true`)
93fk|- `project` (default `true`)
94eg|- `cwd` (default `getProjectDir()`)
95km|- `existingOnly` (default `false`)
96th|
97zh|This API is used for directory-based config lookups (commands, hooks, tools, agents, etc.).
98th|
99hb|## `findConfigFile(subpath, options)` / `findConfigFileWithMeta(...)`
100th|
101mj|Searches for the first existing file across ordered bases, returns first match (path-only or path+metadata).
102nd|
103dv|## `findAllNearestProjectConfigDirs(subpath, cwd)`
104th|
105ig|Walks parent directories upward and returns the **nearest existing directory per source base** (`.omp`, `.claude`, `.codex`, `.gemini`), then sorts results by source priority.
106th|
107vq|Use this when project config should be inherited from ancestor directories (monorepo/nested workspace behavior).
108th|
109zn|---
110th|
111eg|## 3) File config wrapper (`ConfigFile<T>` in `src/config.ts`)
112th|
113hh|`ConfigFile<T>` is the schema-validated loader for single config files.
114th|
115mm|Supported formats:
116th|
117jl|- `.yml` / `.yaml`
118lp|- `.json` / `.jsonc`
119th|
120qu|Behavior:
121st|
122bn|- Validates parsed data with AJV against a provided TypeBox schema.
123ls|- Caches load result until `invalidate()`.
124si|- Returns tri-state result via `tryLoad()`:
125zm|  - `ok`
126mn|  - `not-found`
127mz|  - `error` (`ConfigError` with schema/parse context)
128th|
129vc|Legacy migration still supported:
130th|
131ml|- If target path is `.yml`/`.yaml`, a sibling `.json` is auto-migrated once (`migrateJsonToYml`).
132nd|
133ty|---
134th|
135zy|## 4) Settings resolution model (`src/config/settings.ts`)
136th|
137il|The runtime settings model is layered:
138th|
139ry|1. Global settings: `~/.omp/agent/config.yml`
140xz|2. Project settings: discovered via settings capability (`settings.json` from providers)
141os|3. Runtime overrides: in-memory, non-persistent
142jn|4. Schema defaults: from `SETTINGS_SCHEMA`
143rd|
144fx|Effective read path:
145th|
146zy|`defaults <- global <- project <- overrides`
147th|
148zb|Write behavior:
149th|
150fl|- `settings.set(...)` writes to the **global** layer (`config.yml`) and queues background save.
151fg|- Project settings are read-only from capability discovery.
152nd|
153qx|## Migration behavior still active
154th|
155yj|On startup, if `config.yml` is missing:
156th|
157iw|1. Migrate from `~/.omp/agent/settings.json` (renamed to `.bak` on success)
158sk|2. Merge with legacy DB settings from `agent.db`
159ob|3. Write merged result to `config.yml`
160th|
161xe|Field-level migrations in `#migrateRawSettings`:
162nd|
163wd|- `queueMode` -> `steeringMode`
164tf|- `ask.timeout` milliseconds -> seconds when old value looks like ms (`> 1000`)
165sd|- Legacy flat `theme: "..."` -> `theme.dark/theme.light` structure
166th|
167ot|---
168th|
169ef|## 5) Capability/discovery integration
170th|
171vs|Most non-core config loading flows through the capability registry (`src/capability/index.ts` + `src/discovery/index.ts`).
172nd|
173af|## Provider ordering
174th|
175kb|Providers are sorted by numeric priority (higher first). Example priorities:
176th|
177lf|- Native OMP (`builtin.ts`): `100`
178wc|- Claude: `80`
179ox|- Codex / agents / Claude marketplace: `70`
180vn|- Gemini: `60`
181st|
182xc|```text
183mk|Provider precedence (higher wins)
184th|
185en|native (.omp)          priority 100
186zy|claude                 priority  80
187yk|codex / agents / ...   priority  70
188rf|gemini                 priority  60
189bn|```
190th|
191yl|## Dedup semantics
192nd|
193nu|Capabilities define a `key(item)`:
194th|
195ok|- same key => first item wins (higher-priority/earlier-loaded item)
196yx|- no key (`undefined`) => no dedup, all items retained
197th|
198qa|Relevant keys:
199th|
200fq|- skills: `name`
201ab|- tools: `name`
202gh|- hooks: `${type}:${tool}:${name}`
203bj|- extension modules: `name`
204jc|- extensions: `name`
205ts|- settings: no dedup (all items preserved)
206th|
207qa|---
208th|
209ph|## 6) Native `.omp` provider behavior (`packages/coding-agent/src/discovery/builtin.ts`)
210th|
211tb|Native provider (`id: native`) reads native config from:
212th|
213gc|- project: `<cwd>/.omp/...`
214bu|- user: `~/.omp/agent/...`
215th|
216is|### Directory admission rules
217th|
218mj|- Slash commands, rules, prompts, instructions, hooks, tools, extensions, extension modules, and settings use a project/user root only when the root directory exists and is non-empty.
219hr|- Skills scan `<ancestor>/.omp/skills` for each ancestor from the current working directory up to the repo root/home boundary, plus `~/.omp/agent/skills`, without requiring the root `.omp` directory itself to be non-empty.
220hb|- `SYSTEM.md` and `AGENTS.md` read user-level files directly and use nearest-ancestor project `.omp` lookup for project files, but the project `.omp` directory must be non-empty.
221st|
222by|### Scope-specific loading
223rd|
224pl|- Skills: `<ancestor>/.omp/skills/*/SKILL.md` and `~/.omp/agent/skills/*/SKILL.md`
225wx|- Slash commands: `commands/*.md`
226rc|- Rules: `rules/*.{md,mdc}`
227on|- Prompts: `prompts/*.md`
228wd|- Instructions: `instructions/*.md`
229vn|- Hooks: `hooks/pre/*`, `hooks/post/*`
230sl|- Tools: `tools/*.{json,md,ts,js,sh,bash,py}` and `tools/<name>/index.ts`
231ao|- Extension modules: discovered under `extensions/` (+ legacy `settings.json.extensions` string array)
232ez|- Extensions: `extensions/<name>/gemini-extension.json`
233tk|- Settings capability: `settings.json`
234th|
235oj|### Nearest-project lookup nuance
236th|
237og|## For `SYSTEM.md` and `AGENTS.md`, native provider uses nearest-ancestor project `.omp` directory search (walk-up) and still requires the project `.omp` dir to be non-empty.
238th|
239yd|## 7) How major subsystems consume config
240th|
241lt|## Settings subsystem
242nd|
243rm|- `Settings.init()` loads global `config.yml` + discovered project `settings.json` capability items.
244oz|- Only capability items with `level === "project"` are merged into project layer.
245th|
246ph|## Skills subsystem
247th|
248af|- `extensibility/skills.ts` loads via `loadCapability(skillCapability.id, { cwd })`.
249sz|- Applies source toggles and filters (`ignoredSkills`, `includeSkills`, custom dirs).
250wv|- Legacy-named toggles still exist (`skills.enablePiUser`, `skills.enablePiProject`) but they gate the native provider (`provider === "native"`).
251st|
252sw|## Hooks subsystem
253rd|
254mw|- `discoverAndLoadHooks()` resolves hook paths from hook capability + explicit configured paths.
255qu|- Then loads modules via Bun import.
256th|
257la|## Tools subsystem
258th|
259wo|- `discoverAndLoadCustomTools()` resolves tool paths from tool capability + plugin tool paths + explicit configured paths.
260va|- Declarative `.md/.json` tool files are metadata only; executable loading expects code modules.
261st|
262ju|## Extensions subsystem
263rd|
264sl|- `discoverAndLoadExtensions()` resolves extension modules from extension-module capability plus explicit paths.
265mh|- Current implementation intentionally keeps only capability items with `_source.provider === "native"` before loading.
266th|
267kw|---
268th|
269fm|## 8) Precedence rules to rely on
270th|
271qb|Use this mental model:
272nd|
273qp|1. Source directory ordering from `config.ts` determines candidate path order.
274ah|2. Capability provider priority determines cross-provider precedence.
275zy|3. Capability key dedup determines collision behavior (first wins for keyed capabilities).
276ap|4. Subsystem-specific merge logic can further change effective precedence (especially settings).
277th|
278nn|### Settings-specific caveat
279th|
280ug|Settings capability items are not deduplicated; `Settings.#loadProjectSettings()` deep-merges project items in returned order. Because merge applies later item values over earlier values, effective override behavior depends on provider emission order, not just capability key semantics.
281st|
282dm|---
283rd|
284ih|## 9) Legacy/compatibility behaviors still present
285th|
286le|- `ConfigFile` JSON -> YAML migration for YAML-targeted files.
287il|- Settings migration from `settings.json` and `agent.db` to `config.yml`.
288cr|- Settings key migrations (`queueMode`, `ask.timeout`, flat `theme`, `task.isolation.enabled`, `statusLine.plan_mode`).
289ne|- Legacy setting names `skills.enablePiUser` / `skills.enablePiProject` are still active gates for native skill source.
290th|
291oa|If these compatibility paths are removed in code, update this document immediately; several runtime behaviors still depend on them today.
292nd|

2cv|name: skill-creator
3ka|description: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Claude's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations.
4qn|globs:
5di|  - "**/*"
6oi|alwaysApply: false
7mu|---
8th|
9oy|# Skill Creator
10th|
11mg|This skill provides guidance for creating effective skills.
12th|
13sr|## About Skills
14th|
15lr|Skills are modular, self-contained packages that extend Claude's capabilities by providing
16an|specialized knowledge, workflows, and tools. Think of them as "onboarding guides" for specific
17xx|domains or tasks—they transform Claude from a general-purpose agent into a specialized agent
18kf|equipped with procedural knowledge that no model can fully possess.
19th|
20du|### What Skills Provide
21st|
22fk|1. Specialized workflows - Multi-step procedures for specific domains
23zr|2. Tool integrations - Instructions for working with specific file formats or APIs
24at|3. Domain expertise - Company-specific knowledge, schemas, business logic
25ke|4. Bundled resources - Scripts, references, and assets for complex and repetitive tasks
26th|
27ku|### Anatomy of a Skill
28th|
29um|Every skill consists of a required SKILL.md file and optional bundled resources:
30th|
31bp|```
32kb|skill-name/
33mb|├── SKILL.md (required)
34os|│   ├── YAML frontmatter metadata (required)
35dg|│   │   ├── name: (required)
36tz|│   │   └── description: (required)
37wk|│   └── Markdown instructions (required)
38li|└── Bundled Resources (optional)
39tp|    ├── scripts/          - Executable code (Python/Bash/etc.)
40pp|    ├── references/       - Documentation intended to be loaded into context as needed
41jk|    └── assets/           - Files used in output (templates, icons, fonts, etc.)
42tv|```
43rd|
44kc|#### SKILL.md (required)
45th|
46ue|**Metadata Quality:** The `name` and `description` in YAML frontmatter determine when Claude will use the skill. Be specific about what the skill does and when to use it. Use the third-person (e.g. "This skill should be used when..." instead of "Use this skill when...").
47th|
48fh|#### Bundled Resources (optional)
49th|
50ne|##### Scripts (`scripts/`)
51st|
52sq|Executable code (Python/Bash/etc.) for tasks that require deterministic reliability or are repeatedly rewritten.
53rd|
54mo|- **When to include**: When the same code is being rewritten repeatedly or deterministic reliability is needed
55ts|- **Example**: `scripts/rotate_pdf.py` for PDF rotation tasks
56nu|- **Benefits**: Token efficient, deterministic, may be executed without loading into context
57fd|- **Note**: Scripts may still need to be read by Claude for patching or environment-specific adjustments
58th|
59cg|##### References (`references/`)
60th|
61ax|Documentation and reference material intended to be loaded as needed into context to inform Claude's process and thinking.
62nd|
63nb|- **When to include**: For documentation that Claude should reference while working
64cb|- **Examples**: `references/finance.md` for financial schemas, `references/mnda.md` for company NDA template, `references/policies.md` for company policies, `references/api_docs.md` for API specifications
65ml|- **Use cases**: Database schemas, API documentation, domain knowledge, company policies, detailed workflow guides
66wh|- **Benefits**: Keeps SKILL.md lean, loaded only when Claude determines it's needed
67yw|- **Best practice**: If files are large (>10k words), include grep search patterns in SKILL.md
68yk|- **Avoid duplication**: Information should live in either SKILL.md or references files, not both. Prefer references files for detailed information unless it's truly core to the skill—this keeps SKILL.md lean while making information discoverable without hogging the context window. Keep only essential procedural instructions and workflow guidance in SKILL.md; move detailed reference material, schemas, and examples to references files.
69th|
70ps|##### Assets (`assets/`)
71st|
72pd|Files not intended to be loaded into context, but rather used within the output Claude produces.
73rd|
74nu|- **When to include**: When the skill needs files that will be used in the final output
75ej|- **Examples**: `assets/logo.png` for brand assets, `assets/slides.pptx` for PowerPoint templates, `assets/frontend-template/` for HTML/React boilerplate, `assets/font.ttf` for typography
76mf|- **Use cases**: Templates, images, icons, boilerplate code, fonts, sample documents that get copied or modified
77br|- **Benefits**: Separates output resources from documentation, enables Claude to use files without loading them into context
78th|
79lj|### Progressive Disclosure Design Principle
80th|
81dd|Skills use a three-level loading system to manage context efficiently:
82nd|
83vm|1. **Metadata (name + description)** - Always in context (~100 words)
84ox|2. **SKILL.md body** - When skill triggers (<5k words)
85lm|3. **Bundled resources** - As needed by Claude (Unlimited*)
86th|
87ql|*Unlimited because scripts can be executed without reading into context window.
88th|
89co|## Skill Creation Process
90th|
91ho|To create a skill, follow the "Skill Creation Process" in order, skipping steps only if there is a clear reason why they are not applicable.
92nd|
93ef|### Step 1: Understanding the Skill with Concrete Examples
94th|
95vb|Skip this step only when the skill's usage patterns are already clearly understood. It remains valuable even when working with an existing skill.
96th|
97ej|To create an effective skill, clearly understand concrete examples of how the skill will be used. This understanding can come from either direct user examples or generated examples that are validated with user feedback.
98th|
99ta|For example, when building an image-editor skill, relevant questions include:
100th|
101os|- "What functionality should the image-editor skill support? Editing, rotating, anything else?"
102zk|- "Can you give some examples of how this skill would be used?"
103ng|- "I can imagine users asking for things like 'Remove the red-eye from this image' or 'Rotate this image'. Are there other ways you imagine this skill would be used?"
104xp|- "What would a user say that should trigger this skill?"
105th|
106sl|To avoid overwhelming users, avoid asking too many questions in a single message. Start with the most important questions and follow up as needed for better effectiveness.
107th|
108xy|Conclude this step when there is a clear sense of the functionality the skill should support.
109th|
110cb|### Step 2: Planning the Reusable Skill Contents
111th|
112ql|To turn concrete examples into an effective skill, analyze each example by:
113th|
114ne|1. Considering how to execute on the example from scratch
115gj|2. Identifying what scripts, references, and assets would be helpful when executing these workflows repeatedly
116th|
117ot|Example: When building a `pdf-editor` skill to handle queries like "Help me rotate this PDF," the analysis shows:
118ui|1. Rotating a PDF requires re-writing the same code each time
119oe|2. A `scripts/rotate_pdf.py` script would be helpful to store in the skill
120th|
121lj|Example: When designing a `frontend-webapp-builder` skill for queries like "Build me a todo app" or "Build me a dashboard to track my steps," the analysis shows:
122is|1. Writing a frontend webapp requires the same boilerplate HTML/React each time
123nu|2. An `assets/hello-world/` template containing the boilerplate HTML/React project files would be helpful to store in the skill
124th|
125di|Example: When building a `big-query` skill to handle queries like "How many users have logged in today?" the analysis shows:
126pg|1. Querying BigQuery requires re-discovering the table schemas and relationships each time
127wg|2. A `references/schema.md` file documenting the table schemas would be helpful to store in the skill
128th|
129ga|To establish the skill's contents, analyze each concrete example to create a list of the reusable resources to include: scripts, references, and assets.
130th|
131pe|### Step 3: Initializing the Skill
132nd|
133ux|At this point, it is time to actually create the skill.
134th|
135rk|Skip this step only if the skill being developed already exists, and iteration or packaging is needed. In this case, continue to the next step.
136th|
137nb|When creating a new skill from scratch, always run the `init_skill.py` script. The script conveniently generates a new template skill directory that automatically includes everything a skill requires, making the skill creation process much more efficient and reliable.
138th|
139ql|Usage:
140th|
141pc|```bash
142wk|scripts/init_skill.py <skill-name> --path <output-directory>
143io|```
144th|
145ij|The script:
146th|
147zc|- Creates the skill directory at the specified path
148av|- Generates a SKILL.md template with proper frontmatter and TODO placeholders
149la|- Creates example resource directories: `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/`
150ma|- Adds example files in each directory that can be customized or deleted
151st|
152cj|After initialization, customize or remove the generated SKILL.md and example files as needed.
153rd|
154vz|### Step 4: Edit the Skill
155th|
156lx|When editing the (newly-generated or existing) skill, remember that the skill is being created for another instance of Claude to use. Focus on including information that would be beneficial and non-obvious to Claude. Consider what procedural knowledge, domain-specific details, or reusable assets would help another Claude instance execute these tasks more effectively.
157th|
158cs|#### Start with Reusable Skill Contents
159th|
160yo|To begin implementation, start with the reusable resources identified above: `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` files. Note that this step may require user input. For example, when implementing a `brand-guidelines` skill, the user may need to provide brand assets or templates to store in `assets/`, or documentation to store in `references/`.
161st|
162jg|Also, delete any example files and directories not needed for the skill. The initialization script creates example files in `scripts/`, `references/`, and `assets/` to demonstrate structure, but most skills won't need all of them.
163rd|
164en|#### Update SKILL.md
165th|
166rb|**Writing Style:** Write the entire skill using **imperative/infinitive form** (verb-first instructions), not second person. Use objective, instructional language (e.g., "To accomplish X, do Y" rather than "You should do X" or "If you need to do X"). This maintains consistency and clarity for AI consumption.
167th|
168be|To complete SKILL.md, answer the following questions:
169th|
170yd|1. What is the purpose of the skill, in a few sentences?
171mk|2. When should the skill be used?
172gi|3. In practice, how should Claude use the skill? All reusable skill contents developed above should be referenced so that Claude knows how to use them.
173rd|
174hb|### Step 5: Packaging a Skill
175th|
176ui|Once the skill is ready, it should be packaged into a distributable zip file that gets shared with the user. The packaging process automatically validates the skill first to ensure it meets all requirements:
177th|
178pc|```bash
179uo|scripts/package_skill.py <path/to/skill-folder>
180mu|```
181st|
182nl|Optional output directory specification:
183rd|
184pc|```bash
185rf|scripts/package_skill.py <path/to/skill-folder> ./dist
186bm|```
187th|
188rz|The packaging script will:
189th|
190oe|1. **Validate** the skill automatically, checking:
191uh|   - YAML frontmatter format and required fields
192ov|   - Skill naming conventions and directory structure
193ma|   - Description completeness and quality
194sv|   - File organization and resource references
195th|
196aw|2. **Package** the skill if validation passes, creating a zip file named after the skill (e.g., `my-skill.zip`) that includes all files and maintains the proper directory structure for distribution.
197th|
198du|If validation fails, the script will report the errors and exit without creating a package. Fix any validation errors and run the packaging command again.
199th|
200ob|### Step 6: Iterate
201st|
202qm|After testing the skill, users may request improvements. Often this happens right after using the skill, with fresh context of how the skill performed.
203rd|
204nf|**Iteration workflow:**
205oc|1. Use the skill on real tasks
206vx|2. Notice struggles or inefficiencies
207mv|3. Identify how SKILL.md or bundled resources should be updated
208ex|4. Implement changes and test again

## README

Agent Skills for use with Obsidian.

These skills follow the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) so they can be used by any skills-compatible agent, including Claude Code and Codex CLI.

## Installation

### Marketplace

```
/plugin marketplace add kepano/obsidian-skills
/plugin install obsidian@obsidian-skills
```

### npx skills

```
npx skills add git@github.com:kepano/obsidian-skills.git
```

### Manually

#### Claude Code

Add the contents of this repo to a `/.claude` folder in the root of your Obsidian vault (or whichever folder you're using with Claude Code). See more in the [official Claude Skills documentation](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/overview).

#### Codex CLI

Copy the `skills/` directory into your Codex skills path (typically `~/.codex/skills`). See the [Agent Skills specification](https://agentskills.io/specification) for the standard skill format.

#### OpenCode

Clone the entire repo into the OpenCode skills directory (`~/.opencode/skills/`):

```sh
git clone https://github.com/kepano/obsidian-skills.git ~/.opencode/skills/obsidian-skills
```

Do not copy only the inner `skills/` folder — clone the full repo so the directory structure is `~/.opencode/skills/obsidian-skills/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`.

OpenCode auto-discovers all `SKILL.md` files under `~/.opencode/skills/`. No changes to `opencode.json` or any config file are needed. Skills become available after restarting OpenCode.

## Skills

| Skill | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| [obsidian-markdown](skills/obsidian-markdown) | Create and edit [Obsidian Flavored Markdown](https://help.obsidian.md/obsidian-flavored-markdown) (`.md`) with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and other Obsidian-specific syntax |
| [obsidian-bases](skills/obsidian-bases) | Create and edit [Obsidian Bases](https://help.obsidian.md/bases/syntax) (`.base`) with views, filters, formulas, and summaries |
| [json-canvas](skills/json-canvas) | Create and edit [JSON Canvas](https://jsoncanvas.org/) files (`.canvas`) with nodes, edges, groups, and connections |
| [obsidian-cli](skills/obsidian-cli) | Interact with Obsidian vaults via the [Obsidian CLI](https://help.obsidian.md/cli) including plugin and theme development |
| [defuddle](skills/defuddle) | Extract clean markdown from web pages using [Defuddle](https://github.com/kepano/defuddle-cli), removing clutter to save tokens |

## README

# obsidian-claude-code

An Obsidian plugin that embeds Claude as an AI assistant using the [Claude Agent SDK](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code/tree/main/packages/sdk). Claude gets full access to your vault through the same tools available in Claude Code—read, write, search, and execute commands—plus custom Obsidian-specific actions.

<details>
<summary>Screenshot</summary>

![Chat interface showing Claude searching vault](docs/images/chat-interface.png)

</details>

## What It Does

Claude operates as a persistent assistant in your Obsidian sidebar. Ask questions about your notes, request summaries, or have Claude modify files directly. Conversations persist across sessions, and Claude can resume where you left off.

Built on the Claude Agent SDK, the plugin gives Claude access to built-in tools (Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch) plus Obsidian-specific tools for opening files, executing commands, and navigating the workspace. If you have skills defined in `vault/.claude/skills/`, those load automatically too.

This is a desktop-only plugin. The Claude Agent SDK requires Node.js, which is available in Obsidian's Electron environment but not on mobile.

## Requirements

You need an Anthropic API key or a Claude Max subscription. Both work equally well—the subscription option is convenient if you're already paying for Claude.

## Installation

```bash
cd /path/to/your/vault/.obsidian/plugins
git clone https://github.com/Roasbeef/obsidian-claude-code
cd obsidian-claude-code
bun install && bun run build
```

Then enable the plugin: Obsidian Settings → Community Plugins → obsidian-claude-code.

## Authentication

The plugin supports three authentication methods.

**API Key in Settings.** The simplest option. Open Settings → Claude Code and enter your Anthropic API key. The key is stored locally in Obsidian's plugin data directory.

**Environment Variable.** Set `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` in your shell environment. The plugin reads it automatically.

**Claude Max Subscription.** If you have a Claude Pro or Max subscription, you can use that instead of an API key. Run `claude setup-token` in your terminal to authenticate. This creates a `CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN` that the plugin detects.

For GUI apps like Obsidian to inherit the token on macOS, add this to your shell profile:

```bash
launchctl setenv CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN "$(echo $CLAUDE_CODE_OAUTH_TOKEN)"
```

The settings page shows which authentication method is active.

## Usage

Toggle the sidebar with the ribbon icon or `Cmd+Shift+C`. Type your message and press Enter.

Reference specific files using `@[[filename]]` syntax—the input field provides autocomplete when you type `@`. Type `/` for slash commands: `/new` starts a fresh conversation, `/clear` clears history, `/file` adds the active file to context.

When Claude uses tools, the operations appear as collapsible blocks showing what happened and the result. Write operations display a permission modal unless you've enabled auto-approve in settings.

## Tools and Skills

Claude has access to all built-in Claude Code tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash, Grep, Glob, WebFetch, and WebSearch. These handle file operations, code search, and shell commands.

The plugin also exposes Obsidian-specific tools through an MCP server:

- `open_file` — Open a file in the Obsidian editor
- `execute_command` — Run any Obsidian command
- `show_notice` — Display a notification
- `get_active_file` — Get info about the current file
- `list_commands` — Discover available commands
- `create_note` — Create new notes
- `reveal_in_explorer` — Show a file in the file explorer
- `get_vault_stats` — Query vault statistics
- `get_recent_files` — List recently modified files

### Skills

Skills in `vault/.claude/skills/` load automatically. The repository includes a `vault-search` skill you can copy to your vault:

```bash
cp -r skills/vault-search /path/to/vault/.claude/skills/
```

This skill provides semantic search via sqlite-vec embeddings and SQL queries over note frontmatter (a Dataview alternative). See [skills/README.md](skills/README.md) for setup instructions.

See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for full architectural details.

## Data Storage

Conversations are stored in `.obsidian-claude-code/` at your vault root:

```
.obsidian-claude-code/
├── conversations.json    # Metadata index
└── history/
    └── {id}.json         # Full message history per conversation
```

Add this directory to `.gitignore` if you don't want to sync conversation history.

## Development

```bash
bun run dev      # watch mode with rebuild
bun run build    # production build
make check       # typecheck + lint + test
```

Debug logs are written to `~/.obsidian-claude-code/debug.log`.

## README

# Clausidian

**Claude Code's Obsidian integration** — AI agent toolkit for vault management with zero dependencies. Works seamlessly with Claude Code and all major AI agents.

**Languages:** [English](README.md) | [中文](README.zh.md)

## Documentation

| Document | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| [ARCHITECTURE.md](ARCHITECTURE.md) | System design, cache layers, performance optimization |
| [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) | Version history and release notes |
| [docs/](docs/README.md) | Test plans, feature plans, analysis reports |
| [scaffold/AGENT.md](scaffold/AGENT.md) | Agent quick-reference (commands, conventions) |
| [skill/SKILL.md](skill/SKILL.md) | `/obsidian` skill for Claude Code |

## Claude Code Integration

**One command setup:**

```bash
clausidian setup ~/my-vault
```

This automatically:
1. Installs the `/obsidian` skill to `~/.claude/skills/obsidian/`
2. Registers the MCP server in `~/.claude/.mcp.json`
3. After restart, use `/obsidian` command in Claude Code sessions to manage your vault
4. Registers 44+ MCP tools for programmatic vault operations

All 55+ `clausidian` CLI commands are available as `/obsidian` subcommands.

## Why

AI agents are great at managing knowledge, but they need structure. `clausidian` provides:

- **Structured vault** with frontmatter conventions, templates, and auto-linking
- **CLI interface** that any agent can call — no agent-specific integration needed
- **Automatic indices** — tag index, knowledge graph, directory indexes
- **Agent configs** generated for Claude Code, Cursor, Copilot out of the box

Your agent reads the vault's `AGENT.md`, learns the conventions, and uses `clausidian` CLI to create notes, journals, reviews — all with proper metadata and bidirectional links.

## Install

```bash
npm install -g clausidian
```

## Quick Start

```bash
# Install
npm install -g clausidian

# Initialize a new vault
clausidian init ~/my-vault
cd ~/my-vault

# Setup Claude Code integration (MCP server + /obsidian skill)
clausidian setup ~/my-vault

# Create today's journal
clausidian journal

# Create a project note
clausidian note "Build API" project --tags "backend,api"

# Capture an idea
clausidian capture "Use vector search for note retrieval"

# Search notes
clausidian search "API" --type resource

# List active projects
clausidian list project --status active

# Generate weekly review
clausidian review

# Generate monthly review
clausidian review monthly

# What links to this note?
clausidian backlinks "build-api"

# Update a note's metadata
clausidian update "build-api" --status active --summary "Core API"

# Archive a completed project
clausidian archive "old-project"

# Vault statistics
clausidian stats

# Generate Mermaid knowledge graph
clausidian graph

# Find orphan notes (no inbound links)
clausidian orphans

# Tag management
clausidian tag list
clausidian tag rename "old-tag" "new-tag"

# Rebuild indices
clausidian sync

# Rename a note (updates all references)
clausidian rename "build-api" "API Gateway"

# Move note to a different type
clausidian move "my-idea" project

# Merge two notes
clausidian merge "draft-api" "build-api"

# Find duplicate notes
clausidian duplicates --threshold 0.4

# Find broken links
clausidian broken-links

# Batch operations
clausidian batch tag --type idea --add "needs-review"
clausidian batch archive --tag "deprecated"
clausidian batch update --type project --set-status active

# Export / Import
clausidian export vault-backup.json
clausidian export --format markdown --type project
clausidian import notes.json

# Regex search
clausidian search "API.*v[23]" --regex

# Smart linking
clausidian link --dry-run          # preview missing links
clausidian link                    # create bidirectional links

# Activity timeline
clausidian timeline --days 7
clausidian timeline --type project

# Vault quality
clausidian validate
clausidian relink --dry-run        # preview broken link fixes
clausidian relink                  # auto-fix broken links

# Pin favorites
clausidian pin "important-note"
clausidian pin list
clausidian unpin "important-note"

# Daily dashboard
clausidian daily

# Improvement suggestions
clausidian suggest

# Word count stats
clausidian count
clausidian count --type project

# Pending tasks
clausidian agenda
clausidian agenda --all

# Vault changelog
clausidian changelog --days 14

# Graph exploration
clausidian neighbors "my-project" --depth 3

name: obsidian-cli
description: Interact with Obsidian vaults using the Obsidian CLI to read, create, search, and manage notes, tasks, properties, and more. Also supports plugin and theme development with commands to reload plugins, run JavaScript, capture errors, take screenshots, and inspect the DOM. Use when the user asks to interact with their Obsidian vault, manage notes, search vault content, perform vault operations from the command line, or develop and debug Obsidian plugins and themes.
---

# Obsidian CLI

Use the `obsidian` CLI to interact with a running Obsidian instance. Requires Obsidian to be open.

## Command reference

Run `obsidian help` to see all available commands. This is always up to date. Full docs: https://help.obsidian.md/cli

## Syntax

**Parameters** take a value with `=`. Quote values with spaces:

```bash
obsidian create name="My Note" content="Hello world"
```

**Flags** are boolean switches with no value:

```bash
obsidian create name="My Note" silent overwrite
```

For multiline content use `\n` for newline and `\t` for tab.

## File targeting

Many commands accept `file` or `path` to target a file. Without either, the active file is used.

- `file=<name>` — resolves like a wikilink (name only, no path or extension needed)
- `path=<path>` — exact path from vault root, e.g. `folder/note.md`

## Vault targeting

Commands target the most recently focused vault by default. Use `vault=<name>` as the first parameter to target a specific vault:

```bash
obsidian vault="My Vault" search query="test"
```

## Common patterns

```bash
obsidian read file="My Note"
obsidian create name="New Note" content="# Hello" template="Template" silent
obsidian append file="My Note" content="New line"
obsidian search query="search term" limit=10
obsidian daily:read
obsidian daily:append content="- [ ] New task"
obsidian property:set name="status" value="done" file="My Note"
obsidian tasks daily todo
obsidian tags sort=count counts
obsidian backlinks file="My Note"
```

Use `--copy` on any command to copy output to clipboard. Use `silent` to prevent files from opening. Use `total` on list commands to get a count.

## Plugin development

### Develop/test cycle

After making code changes to a plugin or theme, follow this workflow:

1. **Reload** the plugin to pick up changes:
   ```bash
   obsidian plugin:reload id=my-plugin
   ```
2. **Check for errors** — if errors appear, fix and repeat from step 1:
   ```bash
   obsidian dev:errors
   ```
3. **Verify visually** with a screenshot or DOM inspection:
   ```bash
   obsidian dev:screenshot path=screenshot.png
   obsidian dev:dom selector=".workspace-leaf" text
   ```
4. **Check console output** for warnings or unexpected logs:
   ```bash
   obsidian dev:console level=error
   ```

### Additional developer commands

Run JavaScript in the app context:

```bash
obsidian eval code="app.vault.getFiles().length"
```

Inspect CSS values:

```bash
obsidian dev:css selector=".workspace-leaf" prop=background-color
```

Toggle mobile emulation:

```bash
obsidian dev:mobile on
```

Run `obsidian help` to see additional developer commands including CDP and debugger controls.
