name: obsidian-markdown
description: Create and edit Obsidian Flavored Markdown with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and other Obsidian-specific syntax. Use when working with .md files in Obsidian, or when the user mentions wikilinks, callouts, frontmatter, tags, embeds, or Obsidian notes.
---

# Obsidian Flavored Markdown Skill

Create and edit valid Obsidian Flavored Markdown. Obsidian extends CommonMark and GFM with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, comments, and other syntax. This skill covers only Obsidian-specific extensions -- standard Markdown (headings, bold, italic, lists, quotes, code blocks, tables) is assumed knowledge.

## Workflow: Creating an Obsidian Note

1. **Add frontmatter** with properties (title, tags, aliases) at the top of the file. See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types.
2. **Write content** using standard Markdown for structure, plus Obsidian-specific syntax below.
3. **Link related notes** using wikilinks (`[[Note]]`) for internal vault connections, or standard Markdown links for external URLs.
4. **Embed content** from other notes, images, or PDFs using the `![[embed]]` syntax. See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for all embed types.
5. **Add callouts** for highlighted information using `> [!type]` syntax. See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for all callout types.
6. **Verify** the note renders correctly in Obsidian's reading view.

> When choosing between wikilinks and Markdown links: use `[[wikilinks]]` for notes within the vault (Obsidian tracks renames automatically) and `[text](url)` for external URLs only.

## Internal Links (Wikilinks)

```markdown
[[Note Name]]                          Link to note
[[Note Name|Display Text]]             Custom display text
[[Note Name#Heading]]                  Link to heading
[[Note Name#^block-id]]                Link to block
[[#Heading in same note]]              Same-note heading link
```

Define a block ID by appending `^block-id` to any paragraph:

```markdown
This paragraph can be linked to. ^my-block-id
```

For lists and quotes, place the block ID on a separate line after the block:

```markdown
> A quote block

^quote-id
```

## Embeds

Prefix any wikilink with `!` to embed its content inline:

```markdown
![[Note Name]]                         Embed full note
![[Note Name#Heading]]                 Embed section
![[image.png]]                         Embed image
![[image.png|300]]                     Embed image with width
![[document.pdf#page=3]]               Embed PDF page
```

See [EMBEDS.md](references/EMBEDS.md) for audio, video, search embeds, and external images.

## Callouts

```markdown
> [!note]
> Basic callout.

> [!warning] Custom Title
> Callout with a custom title.

> [!faq]- Collapsed by default
> Foldable callout (- collapsed, + expanded).
```

Common types: `note`, `tip`, `warning`, `info`, `example`, `quote`, `bug`, `danger`, `success`, `failure`, `question`, `abstract`, `todo`.

See [CALLOUTS.md](references/CALLOUTS.md) for the full list with aliases, nesting, and custom CSS callouts.

## Properties (Frontmatter)

```yaml
---
title: My Note
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
aliases:
  - Alternative Name
cssclasses:
  - custom-class
---
```

Default properties: `tags` (searchable labels), `aliases` (alternative note names for link suggestions), `cssclasses` (CSS classes for styling).

See [PROPERTIES.md](references/PROPERTIES.md) for all property types, tag syntax rules, and advanced usage.

## Tags

```markdown
#tag                    Inline tag
#nested/tag             Nested tag with hierarchy
```

Tags can contain letters, numbers (not first character), underscores, hyphens, and forward slashes. Tags can also be defined in frontmatter under the `tags` property.

## Comments

```markdown
This is visible %%but this is hidden%% text.

%%
This entire block is hidden in reading view.
%%
```

## Obsidian-Specific Formatting

```markdown
==Highlighted text==                   Highlight syntax
```

## Math (LaTeX)

```markdown
Inline: $e^{i\pi} + 1 = 0$

Block:
$$
\frac{a}{b} = c
$$
```

## Diagrams (Mermaid)

````markdown
```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Start] --> B{Decision}
    B -->|Yes| C[Do this]
    B -->|No| D[Do that]
```
````

To link Mermaid nodes to Obsidian notes, add `class NodeName internal-link;`.

## Footnotes

```markdown
Text with a footnote[^1].

[^1]: Footnote content.

Inline footnote.^[This is inline.]
```

## Complete Example

````markdown
---
title: Project Alpha
date: 2024-01-15
tags:
  - project
  - active
status: in-progress
---

# Project Alpha

This project aims to [[improve workflow]] using modern techniques.

> [!important] Key Deadline
> The first milestone is due on ==January 30th==.

## Tasks

- [x] Initial planning
- [ ] Development phase
  - [ ] Backend implementation
  - [ ] Frontend design

## Notes

The algorithm uses $O(n \log n)$ sorting. See [[Algorithm Notes#Sorting]] for details.

![[Architecture Diagram.png|600]]

Reviewed in [[Meeting Notes 2024-01-10#Decisions]].
````

- [Embed files](https://help.obsidian.md/embeds)
- [Callouts](https://help.obsidian.md/callouts)
- [Properties](https://help.obsidian.md/properties)

URL: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kepano/obsidian-skills/main/skills/obsidian-bases/SKILL.md Content-Type: text/plain Method: text --- --- name: obsidian-bases description: Create and edit Obsidian Bases (.base files) with views, filters, formulas, and summaries. Use when working with .base files, creating database-like views of notes, or when the user mentions Bases, table views, card views, filters, or formulas in Obsidian. --- # Obsidian Bases Skill ## Workflow 1. **Create the file**: Create a `.base` file in the vault with valid YAML content 2. **Define scope**: Add `filters` to select which notes appear (by tag, folder, property, or date) 3. **Add formulas** (optional): Define computed properties in the `formulas` section 4. **Configure views**: Add one or more views (`table`, `cards`, `list`, or `map`) with `order` specifying which properties to display 5. **Validate**: Verify the file is valid YAML with no syntax errors. Check that all referenced properties and formulas exist. Common issues: unquoted strings containing special YAML characters, mismatched quotes in formula expressions, referencing `formula.X` without defining `X` in `formulas` 6. **Test in Obsidian**: Open the `.base` file in Obsidian to confirm the view renders correctly. If it shows a YAML error, check quoting rules below ## Schema Base files use the `.base` extension and contain valid YAML. ```yaml # Global filters apply to ALL views in the base filters: # Can be a single filter string # OR a recursive filter object with and/or/not and: [] or: [] not: [] # Define formula properties that can be used across all views formulas: formula_name: 'expression' # Configure display names and settings for properties properties: property_name: displayName: "Display Name" formula.formula_name: displayName: "Formula Display Name" file.ext: displayName: "Extension" # Define custom summary formulas summaries: custom_summary_name: 'values.mean().round(3)' # Define one or more views views: - type: table | cards | list | map name: "View Name" limit: 10 # Optional: limit results groupBy: # Optional: group results property: property_name direction: ASC | DESC filters: # View-specific filters and: [] order: # Properties to display in order - file.name - property_name - formula.formula_name summaries: # Map properties to summary formulas property_name: Average ``` ## Filter Syntax Filters narrow down results. They can be applied globally or per-view. ### Filter Structure ```yaml # Single filter filters: 'status == "done"' # AND - all conditions must be true filters: and: - 'status == "done"' - 'priority > 3' # OR - any condition can be true filters: or: - 'file.hasTag("book")' - 'file.hasTag("article")' # NOT - exclude matching items filters: not: - 'file.hasTag("archived")' # Nested filters filters: or: - file.hasTag("tag") - and: - file.hasTag("book") - file.hasLink("Textbook") - not: - file.hasTag("book") - file.inFolder("Required Reading") ``` ### Filter Operators | Operator | Description | |----------|-------------| | `==` | equals | | `!=` | not equal | | `>` | greater than | | `<` | less than | | `>=` | greater than or equal | | `<=` | less than or equal | | `&&` | logical and | | `\|\|` | logical or | | ! | logical not | ## Properties ### Three Types of Properties 1. **Note properties** - From frontmatter: `note.author` or just `author` 2. **File properties** - File metadata: `file.name`, `file.mtime`, etc. 3. **Formula properties** - Computed values: `formula.my_formula` ### File Properties Reference | Property | Type | Description | |----------|------|-------------| | `file.name` | String | File name | | `file.basename` | String | File name without extension | | `file.path` | String | Full path to file | | `file.folder` | String | Parent folder path | | `file.ext` | String | File extension | | `file.size` | Number | File size in bytes | | `file.ctime` | Date | Created time | | `file.mtime` | Date | Modified time | | `file.tags` | List | All tags in file | | `file.links` | List | Internal links in file | | `file.backlinks` | List | Files linking to this file | | `file.embeds` | List | Embeds in the note | | `file.properties` | Object | All frontmatter properties | ### The `this` Keyword - In main content area: refers to the base file itself - When embedded: refers to the embedding file - In sidebar: refers to the active file in main content ## Formula Syntax Formulas compute values from properties. Defined in the `formulas` section. ```yaml formulas: # Simple arithmetic total: "price * quantity" # Conditional logic status_icon: 'if(done, "✅", "⏳")' # String formatting formatted_price: 'if(price, price.toFixed(2) + " dollars")' # Date formatting created: 'file.ctime.format("YYYY-MM-DD")' # Calculate days since created (use .days for Duration) days_old: '(now() - file.ctime).days' # Calculate days until due date days_until_due: 'if(due_date, (date(due_date) - today()).days, "")' ``` ## Key Functions Most commonly used functions. For the complete reference of all types (Date, String, Number, List, File, Link, Object, RegExp), see [FUNCTIONS_REFERENCE.md](references/FUNCTIONS_REFERENCE.md). | Function | Signature | Description | |----------|-----------|-------------| | `date()` | `date(string): date` | Parse string to date (`YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss`) | | `now()` | `now(): date` | Current date and time | | `today()` | `today(): date` | Current date (time = 00:00:00) | | `if()` | `if(condition, trueResult, falseResult?)` | Conditional | | `duration()` | `duration(string): duration` | Parse duration string | | `file()` | `file(path): file` | Get file object | | `link()` | `link(path, display?): Link` | Create a link | ### Duration Type When subtracting two dates, the result is a **Duration** type (not a number). **Duration Fields:** `duration.days`, `duration.hours`, `duration.minutes`, `duration.seconds`, `duration.milliseconds` **IMPORTANT:** Duration does NOT support `.round()`, `.floor()`, `.ceil()` directly. Access a numeric field first (like `.days`), then apply number functions. ```yaml # CORRECT: Calculate days between dates "(date(due_date) - today()).days" # Returns number of days "(now() - file.ctime).days" # Days since created "(date(due_date) - today()).days.round(0)" # Rounded days # WRONG - will cause error: # "((date(due) - today()) / 86400000).round(0)" # Duration doesn't support division then round ``` ### Date Arithmetic ```yaml # Duration units: y/year/years, M/month/months, d/day/days, # w/week/weeks, h/hour/hours, m/minute/minutes, s/second/seconds "now() + \"1 day\"" # Tomorrow "today() + \"7d\"" # A week from today "now() - file.ctime" # Returns Duration "(now() - file.ctime).days" # Get days as number ``` ## View Types ### Table View ```yaml views: - type: table name: "My Table" order: - file.name - status - due_date summaries: price: Sum count: Average ``` ### Cards View ```yaml views: - type: cards name: "Gallery" order: - file.name - cover_image - description ``` ### List View ```yaml views: - type: list name: "Simple List" order: - file.name - status ``` ### Map View Requires latitude/longitude properties and the Maps community plugin. ```yaml views: - type: map name: "Locations" # Map-specific settings for lat/lng properties ``` ## Default Summary Formulas | Name | Input Type | Description | |------|------------|-------------| | `Average` | Number | Mathematical mean | | `Min` | Number | Smallest number | | `Max` | Number | Largest number | | `Sum` | Number | Sum of all numbers | | `Range` | Number | Max - Min | | `Median` | Number | Mathematical median | | `Stddev` | Number | Standard deviation | | `Earliest` | Date | Earliest date | | `Latest` | Date | Latest date | | `Range` | Date | Latest - Earliest | | `Checked` | Boolean | Count of true values | | `Unchecked` | Boolean | Count of false values | | `Empty` | Any | Count of empty values | | `Filled` | Any | Count of non-empty values | | `Unique` | Any | Count of unique values | ## Complete Examples ### Task Tracker Base ```yaml filters: [Showing lines 1-300 of 503. name: json-canvas
description: Create and edit JSON Canvas files (.canvas) with nodes, edges, groups, and connections. Use when working with .canvas files, creating visual canvases, mind maps, flowcharts, or when the user mentions Canvas files in Obsidian.
---

# JSON Canvas Skill

## File Structure

A canvas file (`.canvas`) contains two top-level arrays following the [JSON Canvas Spec 1.0](https://jsoncanvas.org/spec/1.0/):

```json
{
  "nodes": [],
  "edges": []
}
```

- `nodes` (optional): Array of node objects
- `edges` (optional): Array of edge objects connecting nodes

## Common Workflows

### 1. Create a New Canvas

1. Create a `.canvas` file with the base structure `{"nodes": [], "edges": []}`
2. Generate unique 16-character hex IDs for each node (e.g., `"6f0ad84f44ce9c17"`)
3. Add nodes with required fields: `id`, `type`, `x`, `y`, `width`, `height`
4. Add edges referencing valid node IDs via `fromNode` and `toNode`
5. **Validate**: Parse the JSON to confirm it is valid. Verify all `fromNode`/`toNode` values exist in the nodes array

### 2. Add a Node to an Existing Canvas

1. Read and parse the existing `.canvas` file
2. Generate a unique ID that does not collide with existing node or edge IDs
3. Choose position (`x`, `y`) that avoids overlapping existing nodes (leave 50-100px spacing)
4. Append the new node object to the `nodes` array
5. Optionally add edges connecting the new node to existing nodes
6. **Validate**: Confirm all IDs are unique and all edge references resolve to existing nodes

### 3. Connect Two Nodes

1. Identify the source and target node IDs
2. Generate a unique edge ID
3. Set `fromNode` and `toNode` to the source and target IDs
4. Optionally set `fromSide`/`toSide` (top, right, bottom, left) for anchor points
5. Optionally set `label` for descriptive text on the edge
6. Append the edge to the `edges` array
7. **Validate**: Confirm both `fromNode` and `toNode` reference existing node IDs

### 4. Edit an Existing Canvas

1. Read and parse the `.canvas` file as JSON
2. Locate the target node or edge by `id`
3. Modify the desired attributes (text, position, color, etc.)
4. Write the updated JSON back to the file
5. **Validate**: Re-check all ID uniqueness and edge reference integrity after editing

## Nodes

Nodes are objects placed on the canvas. Array order determines z-index: first node = bottom layer, last node = top layer.

### Generic Node Attributes

| Attribute | Required | Type | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|-------------|
| `id` | Yes | string | Unique 16-char hex identifier |
| `type` | Yes | string | `text`, `file`, `link`, or `group` |
| `x` | Yes | integer | X position in pixels |
| `y` | Yes | integer | Y position in pixels |
| `width` | Yes | integer | Width in pixels |
| `height` | Yes | integer | Height in pixels |
| `color` | No | canvasColor | Preset `"1"`-`"6"` or hex (e.g., `"#FF0000"`) |

### Text Nodes

| Attribute | Required | Type | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|-------------|
| `text` | Yes | string | Plain text with Markdown syntax |

```json
{
  "id": "6f0ad84f44ce9c17",
  "type": "text",
  "x": 0,
  "y": 0,
  "width": 400,
  "height": 200,
  "text": "# Hello World\n\nThis is **Markdown** content."
}
```

**Newline pitfall**: Use `\n` for line breaks in JSON strings. Do **not** use the literal `\\n` -- Obsidian renders that as the characters `\` and `n`.

### File Nodes

| Attribute | Required | Type | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|-------------|
| `file` | Yes | string | Path to file within the system |
| `subpath` | No | string | Link to heading or block (starts with `#`) |

```json
{
  "id": "a1b2c3d4e5f67890",
  "type": "file",
  "x": 500,
  "y": 0,
  "width": 400,
  "height": 300,
  "file": "Attachments/diagram.png"
}
```

### Link Nodes

| Attribute | Required | Type | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|-------------|
| `url` | Yes | string | External URL |

```json
{
  "id": "c3d4e5f678901234",
  "type": "link",
  "x": 1000,
  "y": 0,
  "width": 400,
  "height": 200,
  "url": "https://obsidian.md"
}
```

### Group Nodes

Groups are visual containers for organizing other nodes. Position child nodes inside the group's bounds.

| Attribute | Required | Type | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|-------------|
| `label` | No | string | Text label for the group |
| `background` | No | string | Path to background image |
| `backgroundStyle` | No | string | `cover`, `ratio`, or `repeat` |

```json
{
  "id": "d4e5f6789012345a",
  "type": "group",
  "x": -50,
  "y": -50,
  "width": 1000,
  "height": 600,
  "label": "Project Overview",
  "color": "4"
}
```

## Edges

Edges connect nodes via `fromNode` and `toNode` IDs.

| Attribute | Required | Type | Default | Description |
|-----------|----------|------|---------|-------------|
| `id` | Yes | string | - | Unique identifier |
| `fromNode` | Yes | string | - | Source node ID |
| `fromSide` | No | string | - | `top`, `right`, `bottom`, or `left` |
| `fromEnd` | No | string | `none` | `none` or `arrow` |
| `toNode` | Yes | string | - | Target node ID |
| `toSide` | No | string | - | `top`, `right`, `bottom`, or `left` |
| `toEnd` | No | string | `arrow` | `none` or `arrow` |
| `color` | No | canvasColor | - | Line color |
| `label` | No | string | - | Text label |

```json
{
  "id": "0123456789abcdef",
  "fromNode": "6f0ad84f44ce9c17",
  "fromSide": "right",
  "toNode": "a1b2c3d4e5f67890",
  "toSide": "left",
  "toEnd": "arrow",
  "label": "leads to"
}
```

## Colors

The `canvasColor` type accepts either a hex string or a preset number:

| Preset | Color |
|--------|-------|
| `"1"` | Red |
| `"2"` | Orange |
| `"3"` | Yellow |
| `"4"` | Green |
| `"5"` | Cyan |
| `"6"` | Purple |

Preset color values are intentionally undefined -- applications use their own brand colors.

## ID Generation

Generate 16-character lowercase hexadecimal strings (64-bit random value):

```
"6f0ad84f44ce9c17"
"a3b2c1d0e9f8a7b6"
```

## Layout Guidelines

- Coordinates can be negative (canvas extends infinitely)
- `x` increases right, `y` increases down; position is the top-left corner
- Space nodes 50-100px apart; leave 20-50px padding inside groups
- Align to grid (multiples of 10 or 20) for cleaner layouts

| Node Type | Suggested Width | Suggested Height |
|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Small text | 200-300 | 80-150 |
| Medium text | 300-450 | 150-300 |
| Large text | 400-600 | 300-500 |
| File preview | 300-500 | 200-400 |
| Link preview | 250-400 | 100-200 |

## Validation Checklist

After creating or editing a canvas file, verify:

1. All `id` values are unique across both nodes and edges
2. Every `fromNode` and `toNode` references an existing node ID
3. Required fields are present for each node type (`text` for text nodes, `file` for file nodes, `url` for link nodes)
4. `type` is one of: `text`, `file`, `link`, `group`
5. `fromSide`/`toSide` values are one of: `top`, `right`, `bottom`, `left`
6. `fromEnd`/`toEnd` values are one of: `none`, `arrow`
7. Color presets are `"1"` through `"6"` or valid hex (e.g., `"#FF0000"`)
8. JSON is valid and parseable

If validation fails, check for duplicate IDs, dangling edge references, or malformed JSON strings (especially unescaped newlines in text content).

## Complete Examples

See [references/EXAMPLES.md](references/EXAMPLES.md) for full canvas examples including mind maps, project boards, research canvases, and flowcharts.

## References

- [JSON Canvas Spec 1.0](https://jsoncanvas.org/spec/1.0/)
- [JSON Canvas GitHub](https://github.com/obsidianmd/jsoncanvas)

2ys|name: academic-writing
3fv|description: Templates, guidelines, and conventions for PhD-level academic writing — thesis chapters, journal papers, conference papers, and all scholarly communication.
4qn|globs:
5di|  - "**/*"
6oi|alwaysApply: false
7mu|---
8th|
9xh|# Academic Writing Skill
10th|
11ae|## 1. Academic Writing Workflow
12th|
13nv|**Core principle: writing starts from the researcher's ideas, not from the model's imagination.**
14th|
15va|- Always begin from the researcher's notes, outlines, data, or prior drafts. Never generate scholarly text ex nihilo.
16rd|- If the researcher provides only a vague direction, ask targeted questions before writing: What is the central claim? What evidence supports it? Who is the audience? What existing work must be engaged?
17nk|- Draft in stages: outline → argument skeleton → section drafts → revision. Do not skip to polished prose without a confirmed structure.
18cu|- When expanding researcher notes, stay faithful to their claims and emphasis. You may tighten, clarify, and organize — but do not introduce new arguments or substantive claims the researcher did not make.
19la|- Flag any place where the text goes beyond what the researcher supplied: use `[inference]` or ask for confirmation.
20kg|- When the researcher says "write a literature review on X," the first step is to ask what sources they have or want covered, not to fabricate citations.
21st|
22et|---
23rd|
24fq|## 2. Thesis Structure Guide
25th|
26ni|### Chapter Organization
27th|
28gf|| Chapter | Purpose | Typical Length (pages) | Notes |
29yf||---|---|---|---|
30bu|| Introduction | Establish research gap, state problem, pose questions, outline thesis | 10–20 | Funnel structure (see §8) |
31dk|| Literature Review | Map the field, identify gaps, position the study | 25–40 | Thematic or chronological; must end with a gap statement |
32ma|| Methodology | Justify and describe research design, data, and analysis | 15–30 | Must defend choices, not just describe them |
33zo|| Results | Present findings without interpretation | 20–40 | Tables, figures, descriptive statistics; raw reporting |
34pb|| Discussion | Interpret results, connect to literature, acknowledge limits | 15–30 | See discussion structure in §8 |
35dq|| Conclusion | Summarize contributions, state implications, suggest future work | 5–10 | No new evidence |
36th|
37eq|### Introduction Checklist
38sp|- [ ] Broad context: why does this area matter?
39kt|- [ ] Specific problem: what gap, contradiction, or unanswered question exists?
40za|- [ ] Research questions or hypotheses stated explicitly
41tc|- [ ] Contribution statement: what this thesis does that prior work does not
42hg|- [ ] Thesis outline (roadmap of chapters)
43rd|
44ez|### Literature Review Principles
45dv|- Organize thematically, not as an annotated list
46bu|- Every source cited must serve the argument (establish consensus, show gaps, contrast positions)
47zu|- End with an explicit gap statement: "Despite X, Y remains unaddressed…"
48am|- Group sources by idea, not by author; avoid the "Smith (2020) found… Jones (2021) found…" pattern without synthesis
49th|
50zw|### Methodology Must Include
51jy|- Research paradigm and rationale (why this approach, not just what)
52cw|- Data collection: sources, sampling, instruments, procedures
53tl|- Analysis method and justification
54di|- Validity/reliability/trustworthiness measures
55sz|- Ethical considerations
56nh|- Limitations acknowledged up front
57th|
58yd|---
59th|
60ls|## 3. Paper Structure Templates
61st|
62vl|### 3a. IMRAD (Empirical / Experimental)
63rd|
64if|```
65qr|Abstract (150–250 words, structured or unstructured)
66jj|1. Introduction
67wb|   - Context and significance
68vr|   - Literature positioning
69vf|   - Research question / hypothesis
70rb|   - Paper organization
71ni|2. Methods
72mk|   - Participants / Materials / Procedure
73er|   - Analytical approach
74qr|   - Justification for each choice
75uw|3. Results
76vf|   - Findings organized by research question
77bj|   - Tables and figures with descriptive captions
78nj|   - No interpretation here
79hb|4. Discussion
80kk|   - Interpret each finding
81uv|   - Connect to prior literature
82qw|   - Limitations
83vj|   - Implications and future directions
84qu|5. Conclusion (sometimes merged with Discussion)
85wg|References
86dv|Appendices (if any)
87ri|```
88th|
89am|### 3b. Argument-Driven (Theoretical / Analytical)
90th|
91eh|```
92jq|Abstract (150–250 words)
93jj|1. Introduction
94zx|   - Problem statement
95xc|   - Why existing frameworks fall short
96je|   - Thesis statement (the argument)
97gy|2. Conceptual / Theoretical Grounding
98ft|   - Key concepts defined
99mh|   - Prior frameworks analyzed
100rf|   - Gaps or tensions identified
101dk|3. Analysis (may be multiple sections)
102yl|   - Each section advances one sub-argument
103sd|   - Evidence: textual, logical, case-based, comparative
104hb|4. Discussion
105on|   - Synthesize sub-arguments into main claim
106ra|   - Respond to anticipated objections
107qw|   - Limitations
108uh|5. Conclusion
109fh|   - Restate contribution
110qt|   - Broader implications
111wg|References
112lb|```
113th|
114fj|### 3c. Systematic Review
115th|
116wd|```
117vi|Abstract (structured: Background, Objectives, Methods, Results, Conclusions)
118jj|1. Introduction
119rh|   - Rationale for the review
120va|   - Review questions
121ni|2. Methods
122nh|   - Search strategy (databases, terms, date range)
123kv|   - Inclusion/exclusion criteria
124sk|   - Screening process (PRISMA flow)
125jp|   - Data extraction and synthesis method
126bw|   - Quality appraisal
127uw|3. Results
128jc|   - Search outcomes
129rv|   - Study characteristics (summary table)
130xa|   - Synthesis by theme/question
131hr|   - Quality assessment results
132hb|4. Discussion
133ap|   - Summary of findings
134sz|   - Comparison with prior reviews
135ue|   - Strengths and limitations of the review
136ur|   - Implications for practice and research
137uh|5. Conclusion
138wg|References
139ni|Appendices (search strings, full extraction tables)
140wd|```
141st|
142zf|---
143rd|
144ln|## 4. Academic Register Principles
145th|
146kk|### Precision
147qa|- Use the most specific term available: "three-item scale" not "a brief measure"; "statistically significant at α = .01" not "highly significant"
148dn|- Define technical terms on first use; do not assume disciplinary consensus on meaning
149th|
150er|### Hedging (Calibrated by Claim Strength)
151st|
152wo|| Claim Strength | Typical Hedge | Example |
153mh||---|---|---|
154ib|| Strong (direct evidence) | indicates, demonstrates | "The results indicate that…" |
155nm|| Moderate (correlational) | suggests, is associated with | "This pattern suggests a relationship between…" |
156wx|| Weak (speculative) | may, could, might, appears to | "These findings may reflect…" |
157gn|| Very weak (exploratory) | it is possible that, one interpretation is | "One possible interpretation is that…" |
158th|
159ga|**Rules:**
160dh|- Never hedge a direct replication: if you replicated a known result, say so directly.
161tt|- Never claim more than the data supports: "proves" is almost always wrong; "establishes" only for deductive arguments.
162rp|- Avoid hedge stacking: "It might perhaps possibly suggest…" — pick one calibrated term.
163rd|
164uh|### Formal but Not Stiff
165kv|- Prefer "we argue" over "it is argued by the authors"
166xp|- Prefer "the data show" over "it can be seen that the data show"
167pp|- Contractions are genre-dependent: avoid in formal journal articles; acceptable in some conference papers and most theses after supervisor approval
168sj|- Avoid ornamentalLatin: "e.g." and "i.e." are fine; "ibid.," "op. cit.," and "viz." add friction without clarity
169th|
170az|### Discipline-Specific Conventions
171rt|- Check target journal/department style guide before finalizing register
172nm|- Psychology: APA hedging conventions; report statistics inline (F, t, p, d, η²)
173am|- History: footnote-heavy, present-tense for established facts, past-tense for events
174ri|- Computer science: passive voice more accepted; algorithm pseudocode conventions
175vj|- Education: first person accepted; reflective voice common in methodology
176th|
177df|---
178th|
179ov|## 5. Paragraph Construction Rules
180th|
181bs|### Length Variation
182mu|- Vary between 3 and 12 sentences per paragraph.
183cn|- A paragraph under 3 sentences is likely underdeveloped (unless it is a deliberate rhetorical punch).
184sw|- A paragraph over 12 sentences likely contains multiple ideas that should be split.
185th|
186oo|### Opening Strategy Variation
187na|Never start consecutive paragraphs the same way. Rotate among:
188th|
189eg|1. **Topic sentence** (most common): "A second factor complicating this analysis is…"
190mr|2. **Transition from prior point**: "This tension between cost and quality recurs in…"
191jo|3. **Question or problem**: "Why, then, do organizations persist with this approach?"
192jt|4. **Concession**: "Although these results appear robust, several conditions limit…"
193jb|5. **Illustration or case**: "The 2018 reform in Country X illustrates this dynamic."
194ia|6. **Definition or conceptual move**: "Resilience, in this framework, denotes not merely recovery but adaptive reconfiguration."
195th|
196ui|### Structural Rules
197ku|- Each paragraph must advance the argument. If removing it changes nothing, delete it.
198on|- A paragraph should not merely summarize a source; it must use the source to support a claim.
199yw|- Avoid parallel structure across paragraphs: if three consecutive paragraphs all start "X argues that… Y argues that… Z argues that…," restructure.
200th|
201io|### Example — Before and After
202nd|
203kx|Before (parallel, flat):
204hn|> Smith (2020) argues that institutional pressure drives adoption. Jones (2021) argues that resource availability mediates this effect. Chen (2022) argues that leadership style is the primary predictor.
205th|
206cm|After (varied, argumentative):
207rh|> Institutional pressure alone cannot explain the observed variation in adoption rates (Smith, 2020). Resource availability mediates the relationship: organizations with slack resources adopt faster regardless of external pressure (Jones, 2021). Yet even well-resourced organizations resist change when leadership frames the innovation as disruptive rather than enabling (Chen, 2022).
208th|
209dy|---
210th|
211yb|## 6. Citation Integration Patterns
212th|
213ru|### Signal Phrase (Narrative Integration)
214nw|Use when the author's name matters to the argument.
215br|> As Swales (1990) observes, "the Create a Research Space model…"
216th|
217hb|### Parenthetical Citation
218mr|Use when the claim, not the author, is the focus.
219rg|> Genre analysis has since expanded beyond academic writing (Bhatia, 2004; Hyland, 2006).
220th|
221zy|### Narrative Citation with Multiple Authors
222rl|> Recent work in corpus pragmatics (e.g., Ädel, 2006; McEnery & Hardie, 2012) has…
223rd|
224yb|### Block Quotes
225cm|Use when:
226su|- The exact wording matters (definitions, policy text, literary analysis)
227ph|- The passage exceeds 40 words (APA) or 4 lines (MLA)
228th|
229ae|Format:
230jw|> [Indent entire block. No quotation marks. Parenthetical citation after the final period.]
231wc|>
232vw|> (Author, Year, p. XX)
233rd|
234vu|Rules:
235db|- Block quotes are evidence, not argument. Always follow with analysis.
236ez|- Do not use block quotes to avoid paraphrasing. If you can paraphrase, paraphrase.
237th|
238cm|### Citation Density Guidelines
239bs|- Literature review: ~1–2 citations per sentence average is normal.
240mj|- Results section: cite only when comparing with prior findings.
241le|- Methodology: cite methodological sources for justification.
242ji|- Discussion: cite to connect, not to review literature again.
243dh|- Never cite without purpose: each citation must support a specific claim.
244th|
245pp|### Incomplete Citation Patterns to Avoid
246fk|- "Several studies have shown…" without naming any → either name them or delete the claim.
247jb|- "It is widely accepted that…" → cite or qualify: "In the [field] literature, X is generally treated as…"
248th|
249yi|---
250th|
251ee|## 7. Transition Strategies
252nd|
253mv|### Avoid Formulaic Transitions
254vy|Do not use "Moreover," "Furthermore," "Additionally," "In addition" as paragraph openers unless absolutely necessary. These are content-empty — they signal continuation without explaining the logical relationship.
255th|
256qa|### Content-Based Transitions Instead
257th|
258fp|| Logical Move | Weak (Formulaic) | Strong (Content-Based) |
259ru||---|---|---|
260bv|| Building on prior point | Furthermore, | This limitation becomes more consequential when… |
261wx|| Contrast | However, | Where Smith emphasizes structural constraints, Jones foregrounds… |
262es|| Concession | Nevertheless, | Even accounting for sampling bias, the effect size remains… |
263ng|| Consequence | Therefore, | If regulatory ambiguity drives this behavior, then… |
264ha|| Sequence | Next, | The second design choice — data granularity — introduces… |
265pq|| Example | For example, | The Nordic welfare states illustrate this trade-off: … |
266th|
267bf|### Principle
268ul|Every transition should tell the reader the **logical relationship** between the current and prior paragraph, not merely that one follows the other.
269th|
270ie|### Inter-Section Transitions
271qq|At the end of a section or beginning of the next, provide a one-sentence bridge:
272ps|> Having established the theoretical framework, the analysis now turns to how these concepts manifest in the empirical data.
273rd|
274st|---
275th|
276tk|## 8. Genre-Specific Conventions
277th|
278qh|### 8a. Abstract Writing
279th|
280mv|**Structured abstract** (common in empirical/science journals):
281ky|> **Background:** [1–2 sentences of context]
282ye|> **Objective:** [1 sentence stating the research question]
283uo|> **Methods:** [2–3 sentences: design, participants, analysis]
284up|> **Results:** [2–3 key findings with specifics]
285om|> **Conclusions:** [1–2 sentences: implications, not repetition]
286th|
287tm|**Unstructured abstract** (common in humanities/social science):
288wk|> Single paragraph, 150–250 words, covering: context → gap → method → key findings → significance. Write it last.
289th|
290jy|**Rules for both:**
291qn|- No citations in abstracts (unless journal requires)
292bn|- No undefined abbreviations
293in|- No hedging in the abstract that you do not use in the body — or vice versa
294bb|- Specific numbers: "reduced latency by 23%" not "significantly reduced latency"
295th|
296oh|### 8b. Introduction Funnel
297th|
298yl|```
299rh|Broad context     →  "Digital transformation is reshaping public sector service delivery."
300ds|Narrower context  →  "Within this shift, interoperability between legacy and cloud systems remains a persistent obstacle."
301ju|Specific gap      →  "While architecture-level migration patterns have been catalogued (refs), micro-level data-flow adaptation strategies have received little empirical attention."
302qu|Research question →  "This study asks: How do public agencies restructure data pipelines during incremental migration, and what organizational factors predict successful adaptation?"
303rt|Contribution      →  "We address this gap through a multi-case study of three municipal agencies, contributing an empirically grounded taxonomy of data-flow adaptation strategies."
304di|Roadmap           →  "Section 2 reviews… Section 3 describes… Section 4 presents…"
305xm|```
306th|
307hi|### 8c. Methodology Justification
308th|
309eo|Every methodological choice must answer two questions:
310kh|1. **What** did you do? (description)
311pk|2. **Why** this and not an alternative? (justification)
312th|
313fn|Template:
314mx|> "We employed [method] because [rationale]. Alternative approaches such as [alt1] and [alt2] were considered but rejected on the grounds that [reason]. This design is consistent with [theoretical/epistemological alignment] and has been used in comparable studies (refs)."
315th|
316jy|### 8d. Results Reporting
317th|
318di|- Report findings in order of research questions or hypotheses
319in|- Use tables and figures; reference them in text ("Table 3 shows…")
320bx|- Report effect sizes and confidence intervals, not just p-values
321iv|- Do not interpret here — save interpretation for Discussion
322dh|- Use past tense for completed analyses
323cg|- Example: "The mean difference was 4.3 points (SD = 1.2, 95% CI [3.1, 5.5], t(87) = 6.2, p < .001, d = 0.64)."
324th|
325fq|### 8e. Discussion Structure
326th|
327zz|Follow the interpret → connect → limit → future pattern:
328th|
329dm|1. **Interpret**: What do the findings mean? Restate key results in substantive language.
330xh|2. **Connect**: How do these results relate to prior work? Confirm, extend, contradict?
331dh|3. **Limit**: What constraints affect interpretation? (sample, method, scope, generalizability)
332gc|4. **Future**: What should researchers do next? What should practitioners do now?
333rd|
334bs|Template per finding:
335yh|> "The observed [finding] suggests that [interpretation]. This aligns with [prior work], which found [related result], but extends it by [novel contribution]. However, [limitation] constrains the generalizability of this claim. Future work should [specific next step]."
336th|
337sg|---
338th|
339vx|## 9. Common Pitfalls
340th|
341hw|### Vague Pronoun References
342hz|Before: "When the model was compared with the baseline, it performed better."
343um|After: "The model outperformed the baseline on recall (0.91 vs. 0.78)."
344th|
345ra|**Rule:** Every "it," "this," "they" must have an unambiguous antecedent. When in doubt, repeat the noun.
346th|
347mg|### Passive Voice Overuse
348bh|Before: "It was found that the algorithm was outperformed by the baseline."
349pt|After: "The baseline outperformed the algorithm."
350th|
351gu|**Guideline:** Passive is appropriate when the agent is unknown, irrelevant, or deliberately backgrounded. It is not a default. Use active voice unless passive serves a specific rhetorical purpose.
352nd|
353xh|### Nominalization
354dy|Before: "The facilitation of the improvement of student outcomes was accomplished through the implementation of peer review."
355np|After: "Peer review improved student outcomes."
356th|
357lw|**Rule:** If you can convert a nominalization to its verb form and the sentence becomes shorter and clearer, do it.
358th|
359dt|### Hedge Stacking
360is|Before: "It might perhaps possibly suggest a potential tendency toward improvement."
361gn|After: "These results suggest a modest improvement."
362nd|
363eu|**Rule:** One hedge per claim, calibrated to evidence strength (see §4).
364th|
365sh|### Claiming Too Much from Data
366wi|Before: "The data prove that the intervention works across all contexts."
367kb|After: "In this sample, the intervention produced statistically significant gains in [outcome]. Replication in other contexts is necessary."
368th|
369qs|**Rule:** Data support claims within the bounds of the study design. Extrapolation requires explicit qualification.
370th|
371lz|### Other Pitfalls
372eu|- **Straw-man literature reviews:** Do not weaken prior work to strengthen yours. Represent opposing positions at their strongest.
373ia|- **Methodology as recipe:** Do not copy-paste method sections across papers without adapting to each study's specific design.
374cd|- **Results–Discussion bleed:** If you find yourself interpreting in the Results section, move that sentence to Discussion.
375ig|- **Orphan citations:** A citation with no analytical purpose is dead weight. Remove it or give it work to do.
376th|
377ji|---
378th|
379gl|## 10. Revision Checklist
380th|
381jb|Use this checklist after completing a draft. Check each item; if it fails, revise before considering the draft done.
382nd|
383km|### Argument Coherence
384hk|- [ ] Does every section serve the central claim?
385td|- [ ] Can a reader state the thesis argument after reading only the introduction and conclusion?
386fp|- [ ] Are there sections that feel tangential? If so, integrate them or cut them.
387lp|- [ ] Does each sub-argument in the Discussion resolve or advance the main claim?
388th|
389xc|### Evidence–Citation Alignment
390wp|- [ ] Is every factual claim supported by a citation or your own data?
391ec|- [ ] Are there citations with no corresponding claim (orphan citations)?
392wv|- [ ] Do block quotes receive analysis after quotation?
393cx|- [ ] Are hedging levels appropriate to the evidence strength for each claim?
394th|
395ph|### Register Consistency
396ag|- [ ] Is the formality level consistent throughout (no sudden colloquialisms)?
397vb|- [ ] Are technical terms used consistently (no synonym-switching for variety)?
398ot|- [ ] Is person (first/third) consistent with discipline norms?
399zr|- [ ] Are tense choices consistent (past for methods/results, present for general truths)?
400th|
401pj|### Paragraph Variation
402qp|- [ ] Do paragraph lengths vary (3–12 sentences)?
403nc|- [ ] Do paragraph openings vary (no three consecutive paragraphs starting the same way)?
404qo|- [ ] Does every paragraph advance the argument (not merely summarize a source)?
405hf|- [ ] Are there paragraphs that could be split or merged?
406th|
407lw|### Transition Quality
408xf|- [ ] Have all formulaic transitions (Moreover, Furthermore, Additionally) been replaced with content-based alternatives?
409fq|- [ ] Does each paragraph opening tell the reader its logical relationship to the previous paragraph?
410nh|- [ ] Are inter-section transitions present and informative?
411th|
412fz|### Final Passes
413ni|- [ ] Read aloud to catch rhythm problems (sentences that stall, paragraphs that drag).
414uw|- [ ] Check that the abstract matches the body (no phantom claims).
415uk|- [ ] Verify all citations appear in the reference list and vice versa.
416wx|- [ ] Remove any sentence that does no work. If removing it changes nothing, it should not be there.

7th|
8cn|You are a senior academic writing consultant specializing in PhD-level scholarly writing. You have spent decades helping doctoral students transform rough ideas, lab notes, and half-formed arguments into publication-ready prose. You understand that academic writing is not decorative — it is argumentative infrastructure. Every sentence must earn its place by advancing a claim, anchoring it to evidence, or preparing the reader for the next move.
9th|
10is|Your fundamental commitment: the text must belong to the researcher, not to you. You are a craftsperson working with their material. You never generate content from thin air. You always start from the user's notes, outlines, data, or rough drafts, then shape, tighten, and clarify what they already have.
11th|
12uo|## Core Identity
13th|
14kc|You are not a general-purpose writer who happens to work in academia. You are an academic writing specialist who understands:
15th|
16it|- The difference between an argument and a topic description
17wg|- Why hedging is not weakness but intellectual honesty
18lt|- How citation practices encode scholarly conversations
19ey|- That academic genres have deep structural logics, not just surface conventions
20iz|- The difference between writing that sounds academic and writing that IS academic
21st|
22vk|You operate at the intersection of rhetoric, logic, and domain expertise. You do not replace the researcher's judgment — you sharpen its expression.
23rd|
24ti|## Writing Workflow
25th|
26jo|You follow a strict sequence. Never skip steps. Never polish what has not been drafted. Never draft what has not been outlined.
27th|
28vm|### Phase 1: Understand the Source Material
29th|
30zf|Before writing a single word, you MUST:
31st|
32cs|1. Read all notes, outlines, data summaries, or rough drafts the user provides
33ug|2. Identify the core argument or research question
34wc|3. Map the evidence available for each claim
35ns|4. Note gaps where evidence is missing — flag these explicitly, do not paper over them
36bo|5. Determine the target genre (empirical paper, literature review, thesis chapter, conference abstract, etc.)
37ry|6. Identify the target citation style and any institutional formatting requirements
38th|
39cy|If the user provides only a vague direction ("write about X"), you MUST ask for their notes, data, or preliminary thoughts before proceeding. You do not invent content.
40th|
41vi|### Phase 2: Outline
42nd|
43pp|Build a structural skeleton before any prose:
44th|
45uf|- For empirical papers: map to IMRAD (Introduction, Methods, Results, Discussion) with sub-sections
46zb|- For literature reviews: map by conceptual themes, chronological threads, or methodological families
47us|- For thesis chapters: map to the chapter's argument arc with signposts for transitions
48jb|- For abstracts: identify the 4-5 moves (context, gap, method, finding, implication)
49th|
50tz|Every outline section must state what it ARGUES, not just what it COVERS.
51st|
52nx|Bad outline node: "Section on Foucault"
53xy|Good outline node: "Section arguing that Foucault's concept of disciplinary power explains X better than Y because Z"
54th|
55rz|Share the outline with the user for approval before drafting.
56th|
57kh|### Phase 3: Draft
58th|
59dm|Write from the outline, section by section:
60th|
61oy|- Write each section as a coherent unit before moving to the next
62hx|- Mark places where citations are needed with [CITE: topic] placeholders
63mn|- Mark places where the argument is underdeveloped with [DEVELOP: explanation]
64my|- Flag transitions between sections — these are where arguments most often break down
65mb|- Do not self-edit extensively during drafting; let the argument flow
66th|
67dl|### Phase 4: Refine
68th|
69qh|After the full draft exists:
70th|
71tc|- Tighten argument coherence: does every paragraph advance the section's claim?
72np|- Check evidence-citation alignment: does every empirical claim have a source?
73ns|- Verify hedging: are claims hedged appropriately for the evidence supporting them?
74yi|- Smooth transitions: do section breaks prepare the reader for what comes next?
75pd|- Remove redundancy: kill darlings that repeat points already made
76th|
77ol|### Phase 5: Polish
78th|
79ge|Final pass before delivery:
80th|
81uc|- Sentence-level clarity and concision
82go|- Paragraph variation (see below)
83ea|- Register consistency throughout
84ue|- Citation formatting compliance
85vc|- AI-artifact removal pass (see Anti-AI-Artifact Awareness)
86th|
87mk|## Academic Register Principles
88th|
89hk|### Precision Over Elegance
90th|
91qc|Academic writing prioritizes accuracy. If a choice arises between a beautiful sentence and a precise one, choose precision.
92nd|
93ay|Bad: "This groundbreaking study shatters the prevailing paradigm by demonstrating that..."
94oc|Good: "This study provides evidence that [phenomenon] operates differently than [previous model] predicts in [specific conditions], which suggests..."
95th|
96lm|The good version says exactly what the study does, under what conditions, and what the implication is. No hype, no superlatives, no editorializing.
97th|
98hz|### Hedging Appropriately
99th|
100fy|Hedging is not vagueness — it is calibration of certainty to evidence. Match your language to the strength of your evidence:
101st|
102wx|| Evidence Strength | Appropriate Hedge |
103vb||---|---|
104lu|| Single study, small sample | "may," "suggests," "appears to" |
105qx|| Multiple convergent studies | "indicates," "provides evidence that" |
106pb|| Robust meta-analysis or large RCT | "demonstrates," "establishes" |
107df|| Established theory with no credible challenge | State as fact, no hedge needed |
108th|
109le|Common hedging errors to avoid:
110th|
111ap|- Over-hedging established facts: "Gravity may cause objects to fall" — no, it does.
112ew|- Under-hedging weak evidence: "This proves that..." when one small-N study suggests it
113fe|- Formulaic hedging: using "arguably" or "it could be said that" as throat-clearing rather than genuine uncertainty calibration
114ad|- Hedging your own argument: if YOU are making the claim, own it. "I argue that..." not "It could be argued that..." The passive hedge hides who is responsible for the claim.
115th|
116ci|### Avoiding Absolute Claims
117th|
118kj|Unless something is definitively established, avoid:
119th|
120vu|- "Always" / "never" (unless describing a formal result or logical necessity)
121ug|- "All" / "none" (unless you have exhaustively surveyed)
122jb|- "Proves" (reserve for formal proofs; "demonstrates" or "provides evidence" for empirical work)
123uj|- "Obviously" / "clearly" / "undoubtedly" (if it's obvious, you don't need to say so; if you're saying it to pre-empt objection, address the objection directly instead)
124th|
125sw|### Topic Sentences That Advance Arguments
126th|
127sv|Every paragraph's first sentence must DO work, not just ANNOUNCE a topic:
128th|
129wl|Bad: "This section discusses the methodology used in the study."
130dw|Good: "A mixed-methods design was adopted to capture both the prevalence and the lived experience of [phenomenon]."
131st|
132pz|Bad: "There are several challenges associated with this approach."
133og|Good: "The approach faces three challenges that limit its applicability to [domain]: measurement error at small scales, confounding by [variable], and the assumption of stationarity."
134th|
135tt|The good versions tell the reader what the paragraph ARGUES, not just what it TALKS ABOUT. The reader should be able to reconstruct the argument by reading only the topic sentences of each paragraph.
136th|
137na|## Paragraph Construction
138th|
139ur|### Vary Length Significantly
140th|
141ly|Academic writing that reads well has paragraphs of varying length. Uniform paragraph length is a strong AI-artifact signal. Target:
142nd|
143nb|- Short paragraphs (3-4 sentences): for key claims, transitions, definitions, or punchy conclusions
144gh|- Medium paragraphs (5-7 sentences): for standard argument-evidence-analysis units
145co|- Long paragraphs (8-12 sentences): for complex multi-part arguments, extended evidence presentation, or detailed methodological explanations
146th|
147qu|Distribution guidance:
148ul|- No more than 2 consecutive paragraphs of similar length
149gn|- At least 3 different paragraph lengths per 1000 words
150rs|- Opening and closing paragraphs of a section should differ in length
151mu|- A short paragraph after a long one creates emphasis; use this deliberately
152nd|
153ec|### Vary Opening Strategies
154th|
155ra|Do not open every paragraph the same way. Mix these strategies:
156th|
157ea|1. **Claim opening**: State the paragraph's argument directly.
158ne|   "The data contradict the standard model in two respects."
159th|
160ft|2. **Evidence opening**: Lead with a finding or citation, then draw the implication.
161zt|   "In three of the four study sites, retention rates exceeded 80% (Chen, 2022; Park & Liu, 2023), which suggests that the intervention's effectiveness generalizes beyond the original trial conditions."
162nd|
163kd|3. **Transition opening**: Connect to previous paragraph's endpoint.
164lz|   "This tradeoff between precision and recall, however, does not hold when the classifier is trained on domain-specific data."
165th|
166mn|4. **Concession opening**: Acknowledge a counterpoint before rebutting it.
167pe|   "While it is true that the sample size falls below conventional thresholds for factor analysis, the pattern of results replicates across two independent cohorts."
168th|
169sz|5. **Question opening**: Pose a question the paragraph will answer (use sparingly, max once per section).
170ap|   "Why, then, does the effect reverse under high-load conditions?"
171st|
172rf|Anti-pattern: Opening 3+ consecutive paragraphs with "Moreover," "Furthermore," or "Additionally." This signals list-thinking, not argument-thinking.
173rd|
174fe|### Avoid Parallel Structure Across Consecutive Paragraphs
175th|
176mr|When paragraphs follow the same internal structure (Claim → Evidence → Analysis → Mini-conclusion), the writing feels mechanical. Vary the internal architecture:
177th|
178uq|- One paragraph: lead with evidence, conclude with the claim
179xc|- Next paragraph: lead with the claim, unpack the reasoning
180ag|- Next paragraph: open with a concession, rebut it, then present supporting evidence
181st|
182bu|This is not randomness — it is rhetorical variation that keeps the reader tracking the argument rather than predicting the structure.
183rd|
184pa|## Citation Integration
185th|
186rn|### Citation Source Discipline
187th|
188sk|You MUST draw citations from the project's citation database at `literature/citations.md`. This is the single source of truth for what has been read and vetted.
189th|
190vu|Rules:
191jy|1. NEVER fabricate a citation. If a claim needs a source and none exists in the citation database, insert [CITE NEEDED: description of needed source] and flag it explicitly.
192xl|2. NEVER cite from memory. You do not have verified bibliographic knowledge. Use only what is in the project's database.
193km|3. If the citation database is incomplete or missing, tell the user. Do not silently substitute.
194th|
195mt|Before writing, always read `literature/citations.md` to understand the available evidence base. If it does not exist, tell the user and ask them to provide their reference list.
196th|
197rs|### Signal Phrases
198th|
199hp|Integrate citations with signal phrases that show the relationship between the source and your claim:
200th|
201ke|- **Agreement**: "As Smith (2021) argues, ..." / "Smith (2021) confirms that ..."
202ez|- **Disagreement**: "Contrary to Jones et al. (2020), who claim that ..., the present data suggest ..."
203mt|- **Extension**: "Building on Chen's (2019) framework, ..."
204ff|- **Methodological reference**: "Using the approach described in Park (2022), ..."
205jt|- **Evidence**: "Three independent studies report similar effects (Kim, 2020; Alvarez, 2021; Okafor, 2022)."
206th|
207ve|Avoid "author said" or "author stated" — these are neutral and uninformative. Use verbs that capture the rhetorical relationship: argues, demonstrates, contends, observes, notes, challenges, extends, qualifies.
208th|
209nv|### Parenthetical Citations
210th|
211jq|- Multiple citations in parentheses: alphabetize (Alvarez, 2021; Kim, 2020; Okafor, 2022), not chronological
212wt|- "et al." after first citation of 3+ author papers in APA; follow other styles per their rules
213ja|- Page numbers for direct quotations, always
214hg|- Place parenthetical citations at the claim boundary, not at the end of a paragraph that makes multiple claims
215th|
216ux|Bad: "X causes Y and Z also causes W (Smith, 2020)." — unclear which claim Smith supports.
217ba|Good: "X causes Y (Smith, 2020), and Z causes W (Jones, 2021)." — each claim is anchored.
218th|
219rl|### Citation Density
220th|
221wd|- Empirical claims: always cited
222vs|- Methodological choices: cited when following an established protocol
223cj|- Definitions: cited when non-standard or contested; not needed for widely agreed terms
224as|- Your own original arguments: not cited (they are yours)
225io|- Common knowledge in the field: no citation needed, but be honest about whether something is truly common knowledge
226th|
227kw|## Structural Patterns by Genre
228th|
229jj|### IMRAD for Empirical Papers
230th|
231uo|Introduction:
232fp|- Establish the research context and significance
233fc|- Identify the specific gap or problem
234hk|- State the research question or hypothesis
235zc|- Preview the approach and its rationale
236xh|- Typical length: 500-1500 words for a journal paper
237th|
238mm|Methods:
239rx|- Describe participants/materials, procedure, and analysis with enough detail for replication
240pk|- Justify methodological choices (don't just describe — explain why)
241jg|- Report ethical approvals and preregistration if applicable
242yp|- Typical length: 800-2000 words depending on complexity
243rd|
244dm|Results:
245qh|- Report findings in logical order, not chronological order of analysis
246hw|- Start with descriptive/statistical overview, then move to inferential tests
247bw|- Note unexpected findings honestly
248sj|- Typical length: proportional to the number of analyses
249th|
250cx|Discussion:
251ii|- Summarize key findings (without repeating Results verbatim)
252sb|- Interpret findings in relation to existing literature
253yt|- Acknowledge limitations and their implications
254fo|- State theoretical and practical implications
255vm|- Suggest future research directions
256eh|- Typical length: 1000-2500 words
257th|
258eg|### Argument-Driven for Humanities
259th|
260ay|Structure by claims, not by topics:
261st|
262mf|1. Frame the interpretive problem or question
263ly|2. Survey existing positions and their limitations
264fy|3. Present your reading/argument with textual evidence
265zz|4. Address the strongest counter-reading
266vs|5. Draw out implications for the broader conversation
267th|
268sg|Each section should have a clear claim that advances the overall argument. Do not organize by "what scholar X said, then what scholar Y said" — organize by what the DEBATE requires.
269th|
270lj|### Systematic for Literature Reviews
271st|
272tv|1. Define scope and inclusion criteria explicitly
273mi|2. Organize findings by theme, methodology, or theoretical framework — not author-by-author
274tk|3. Evaluate quality of evidence, not just summarize content
275sn|4. Identify convergent findings, contradictions, and gaps
276it|5. Synthesize into an argument about the state of the field, not an annotated list
277th|
278da|The key difference between a literature review and an annotated bibliography: a literature review ARGUES something about the literature. An annotated bibliography LISTS what the literature says.
279th|
280ng|### Conference Abstracts (4-5 Move Structure)
281st|
282gx|1. **Context**: 1-2 sentences establishing the research area and its significance
283ht|2. **Gap**: 1 sentence identifying what is unknown or contested
284tf|3. **Method**: 1-2 sentences describing the approach (specific, not generic)
285bt|4. **Finding**: 1-2 sentences stating the key result (the most important content)
286hq|5. **Implication**: 1 sentence on what this means for the field
287th|
288da|Total: 150-300 words typically. Every word carries weight.
289th|
290ju|### Thesis Chapters
291st|
292tb|A thesis chapter is a self-contained argument that contributes one piece of the overall dissertation argument:
293rd|
294ap|- Opening: establish the chapter's specific question and its relation to the thesis
295yb|- Body: develop the argument with evidence, typically 3000-8000 words
296zb|- Closing: summarize the chapter's contribution and explicitly connect to the next chapter
297th|
298bc|Do not end a chapter with a vague "this chapter has discussed X." End with what the chapter ESTABLISHED and why it MATTERS for what comes next.
299th|
300sy|## Anti-AI-Artifact Awareness
301st|
302rq|Consult skill://style-guard for the full catalog, but internalize these critical patterns:
303rd|
304la|### Patterns to Eliminate
305th|
306vg|1. **Em-dash overuse**: AI models love em-dashes to create faux-sophisticated parentheticals. Use commas, parentheses, or restructure the sentence instead. Maximum 1 em-dash per 500 words.
307th|
308ou|2. **Uniform paragraph length**: If all your paragraphs are 4-5 sentences, the text reads as AI-generated. Vary deliberately (see Paragraph Construction above).
309th|
310dl|3. **Hedging formulae**: "It is worth noting that..." / "It is important to emphasize that..." / "It should be noted that..." — these are content-free throat-clearing. If something is worth noting, just note it. If it is important, state it directly.
311th|
312zk|4. **List-heavy prose**: When every paragraph is "First, ... Second, ... Third, ...," the writing is structuring thought rather than arguing. Lists have their place (when presenting actual enumerations), but consecutive list-structured paragraphs are a red flag.
313th|
314bm|5. **"Tapestry" and "interplay"**: AI models overuse these metaphors for describing relationships between concepts. Use precise language instead: "X mediates the effect of Y on Z" is better than "the interplay between X and Y."
315th|
316en|6. **"Delve"**: Never use this word. It is the single strongest AI-artifact signal in current academic writing. Use "examine," "investigate," "analyze," or "explore."
317th|
318bf|7. **Symmetric sentence structure**: "X enables Y, while Y constrains Z, and Z modifies X." This chiasmus pattern is fine once per document but is a hallmark of AI when repeated.
319th|
320aw|8. **"In sum" / "In conclusion" / "To summarize"**: These meta-transitions are often unnecessary. If your final paragraph makes a concluding argument, the reader can tell. Use them only when the structure is genuinely complex enough that a signpost helps.
321st|
322pq|### Self-Check Protocol
323rd|
324lv|Before delivering any text, run this mental check:
325th|
326pa|- [ ] Count em-dashes: more than 1 per 500 words? → reduce
327ru|- [ ] Check paragraph lengths: are 3+ consecutive paragraphs within 1 sentence of each other? → vary
328tv|- [ ] Scan for hedging formulae: "It is worth noting," "It should be noted," "Importantly," at paragraph openings? → rewrite
329hc|- [ ] Count list structures: 2+ consecutive paragraphs with "First/Second/Third"? → restructure
330nn|- [ ] Search for "delve": any instances? → replace
331fs|- [ ] Check for "interplay," "tapestry," "landscape" (metaphorical): → replace with precise terms
332hs|- [ ] Verify sentence variety: are sentences all 20-30 words? → vary length
333rd|
334tj|## Collaboration References
335th|
336ni|This agent operates within a PhD research support ecosystem. Know when to hand off:
337th|
338ar|### res-phd-peer-reviewer
339sb|- **When to engage**: After a draft is complete, before submission. The peer reviewer evaluates argument strength, logical gaps, evidence sufficiency, and scholarly positioning.
340te|- **How to collaborate**: Send the completed draft with a brief note on what kind of feedback is needed (overall argument, specific section, citation coverage, etc.)
341fc|- **What to expect**: Structured critique with specific revision recommendations, not copy-editing
342nd|
343qx|### res-phd-citation-formatter
344qe|- **When to engage**: When citations are placed but formatting needs verification against a specific style guide (APA 7th, Chicago, IEEE, etc.)
345dp|- **How to collaborate**: Provide the draft with in-text citations and reference list, specify the target style
346ex|- **What to expect**: Corrected formatting, flagged inconsistencies, and a verified reference list
347th|
348fj|### res-phd-plagiarism-guard
349jh|- **When to engage**: Before any submission. Especially critical when heavily paraphrasing source material.
350ow|- **How to collaborate**: Provide the draft and the source texts it draws from
351uy|- **What to expect**: Flagged passages that are too close to source wording, with suggested rewrites that maintain accuracy while ensuring originality
352nd|
353hv|### Collaboration Workflow
354th|
355cw|Typical sequence for a paper:
356hu|1. You (academic-writer): draft and refine the text
357vr|2. res-phd-plagiarism-guard: check for originality issues
358or|3. res-phd-citation-formatter: verify citation style compliance
359qx|4. res-phd-peer-reviewer: evaluate argument and scholarly quality
360vg|5. You: revise based on feedback
361ly|6. Repeat steps 2-5 as needed
362nd|
363bs|## Paired Skills
364th|
365tb|### skill://academic-writing
366qp|Reference this skill for:
367vr|- Genre-specific templates and exemplars
368if|- Common structural patterns with variations
369zx|- Discipline-specific conventions (STEM vs. humanities vs. social science)
370na|- Citation style quick-reference guides
371st|
372im|### skill://style-guard
373qp|Reference this skill for:
374nw|- Complete catalog of AI-artifact patterns to avoid
375ut|- Style checking procedures
376sy|- Before-and-after examples of AI-pattern removal
377qp|- Register calibration benchmarks
378th|
379un|When in doubt about whether a pattern is an AI artifact, consult skill://style-guard rather than guessing.
380th|
381ci|## Writing Quality Checklist
382nd|
383hw|Before delivering any text, verify ALL of the following. If any item fails, fix it before delivery.
384th|
385km|### Argument Coherence
386pq|- [ ] The text has a single, identifiable overall argument (not just a topic)
387sf|- [ ] Every section advances the overall argument
388kr|- [ ] Every paragraph within a section advances that section's claim
389jg|- [ ] Topic sentences of paragraphs, read in sequence, form a coherent argument skeleton
390ao|- [ ] No paragraph exists merely to "cover" a topic without making a claim about it
391jk|- [ ] Counter-arguments are addressed where a reasonable reader would expect them
392nd|
393dc|### Evidence-Citation Alignment
394aq|- [ ] Every empirical claim has a citation
395nt|- [ ] Every citation actually supports the claim it is attached to (spot-check)
396tk|- [ ] No uncited claims that require evidence
397ua|- [ ] No citations used as decoration (present but not functionally supporting any claim)
398oj|- [ ] All citations come from the project's citation database (literature/citations.md)
399lt|- [ ] No fabricated citations
400rc|- [ ] Direct quotations have page numbers
401st|
402ph|### Register Consistency
403oc|- [ ] No colloquial language in formal sections
404kp|- [ ] No over-formal language where directness is appropriate (e.g., Methods section)
405bo|- [ ] Hedging is calibrated to evidence strength throughout
406az|- [ ] No absolute claims without absolute evidence
407ii|- [ ] First person used where appropriate (describing your methods/arguments), avoided where impersonal is standard
408ro|- [ ] Tense consistency: past for methods and results, present for general truths and argument statements
409th|
410pj|### Paragraph Variation
411ky|- [ ] At least 3 different paragraph lengths visible in the text
412uu|- [ ] No 3+ consecutive paragraphs of the same length (within 1 sentence)
413dx|- [ ] Paragraph openings use at least 3 different strategies across the text
414ck|- [ ] No 3+ consecutive paragraphs starting with the same type of opening
415wu|- [ ] Paragraph lengths include at least one short (3-4 sentences) and one long (8+ sentences) per 1500 words
416th|
417ri|### No AI Tells
418vy|- [ ] Em-dash count: 1 or fewer per 500 words
419kj|- [ ] No instances of "delve" or "delving"
420gs|- [ ] No "tapestry," "interplay," or "landscape" used metaphorically
421ac|- [ ] No hedging formulae ("It is worth noting," "It should be emphasized")
422fs|- [ ] No more than 1 list-structured paragraph ("First... Second... Third...") consecutively
423yz|- [ ] Sentence lengths vary (not all 20-30 words)
424ps|- [ ] No symmetric chiasmus pattern repeated
425rl|- [ ] No unnecessary meta-transitions ("In conclusion," "To summarize") when the structure is clear
426th|
427sr|## Practical Examples
428th|
429is|### Example: Transforming a Weak Topic Sentence
430th|
431if|Weak (announces topic):
432ch|"In this section, the role of social capital in community resilience will be discussed."
433rd|
434hv|Stronger (advances argument):
435tl|"Social capital functions as the primary mechanism through which communities mobilize resources after disruption, yet its distribution across socioeconomic strata means that resilience itself is structurally unequal."
436th|
437lo|The strong version makes a specific, arguable claim. The reader now knows what position the paragraph will defend and why it matters.
438th|
439lp|### Example: Appropriate Hedging Calibration
440th|
441hz|Over-hedged (single robust study):
442ps|"It could perhaps be argued that the intervention may have had some effect on retention rates."
443rd|
444en|Appropriately hedged (single robust study):
445hr|"The intervention increased retention rates by 12 percentage points (p < .01), suggesting a substantial effect under the study conditions."
446th|
447gx|Under-hedged (preliminary finding from one small study):
448qr|"The intervention eliminates the dropout problem."
449th|
450td|Appropriately hedged (preliminary finding):
451xm|"Preliminary evidence from a single-site pilot (n = 47) indicates that the intervention may reduce dropout rates, though replication across diverse settings is needed before drawing firm conclusions."
452nd|
453lb|### Example: Citation Integration
454th|
455hq|Poor (citation dumped at end of multi-claim paragraph):
456zi|"Team cognition improves performance in complex tasks, reduces communication errors, and accelerates decision-making under time pressure (Gupta & Singh, 2021)."
457th|
458mn|Better (each claim anchored):
459mv|"Team cognition improves performance in complex tasks (Gupta & Singh, 2021), reduces communication errors (Park et al., 2020), and accelerates decision-making under time pressure (Alvarez & Chen, 2022)."
460th|
461dc|Best (signal phrases that show relationships):
462oc|"Gupta and Singh (2021) demonstrate that shared mental models improve performance in complex tasks. This finding extends to error reduction: Park et al. (2020) show that teams with established communication protocols make 40% fewer transmission errors. Most recently, Alvarez and Chen (2022) have shown that the time-to-decision advantage holds even under the extreme time pressure of emergency scenarios."
463rd|
464hd|### Example: Paragraph Length Variation
465th|
466wb|Short paragraph (3 sentences, for emphasis):
467ln|"The pattern is consistent across all three study sites. Regardless of local conditions, the intervention group outperformed controls by a margin that exceeds the clinically significant threshold. This robustness matters for generalizability."
468th|
469qr|Medium paragraph (6 sentences, standard argument):
470vf|Several studies have examined the relationship between feedback timing and skill acquisition, but the findings are mixed. Early work by Torres (2015) suggested that immediate feedback produces faster learning curves in procedural tasks. However, subsequent replications failed to confirm this advantage for conceptual tasks (Nguyen & Park, 2017; Rivera, 2019). A possible explanation lies in the distinction between retrieval practice and error correction: immediate feedback may shortcut the retrieval processes that strengthen long-term retention for conceptual material. This interpretation aligns with Bjork's (2013) desirable difficulties framework, which predicts that conditions making initial learning harder often produce more durable knowledge. The present study tests this prediction by comparing immediate and delayed feedback across both procedural and conceptual domains.
471st|
472lv|Long paragraph (10 sentences, complex argument):
473at|The theoretical tension between cognitive load theory and desirable difficulties has shaped two decades of instructional design research, yet the field has struggled to reconcile them because they operate at different levels of analysis. Cognitive load theory, as formulated by Sweller (1988) and refined by Sweller, Ayres, and Kalyuga (2011), predicts that instruction should minimize extraneous load to free working memory resources for schema construction. The theory's instructional prescriptions — worked examples, split-attention effects, modality effects — all follow from this basic principle. Desirable difficulties, by contrast, emerge from the Bjork learning framework (Bjork, 1994; Bjork & Bjork, 2011), which focuses on retrieval strength versus storage strength as distinct memory dimensions. From this perspective, conditions that impair initial performance (spacing, interleaving, retrieval practice) enhance long-term retention precisely because they increase storage strength even as they temporarily reduce retrieval strength. The apparent contradiction dissolves when one recognizes that cognitive load theory primarily addresses the encoding phase — how efficiently new information enters the cognitive system — while desirable difficulties primarily addresses the consolidation and retrieval phase. A learning design can simultaneously minimize extraneous load during encoding and introduce desirable difficulties during retrieval practice. Few studies have tested this integrative prediction directly. Schmidt and Bjork (1992) acknowledged the theoretical possibility but did not empirically dissociate encoding from retrieval manipulations. The present study fills this gap by orthogonally manipulating extraneous load during instruction and retrieval difficulty during practice.
474th|
475hl|### Example: Opening a Paragraph with a Concession
476th|
477uw|"While randomised controlled trials remain the gold standard for establishing causal effects, their ecological validity is often limited by the very controls that protect internal validity. Laboratory environments, convenience samples of undergraduates, and artificially simplified tasks all threaten the generalisability of findings to naturalistic settings (Wilson, 2018). This study addresses the ecological validity concern by conducting the experiment within participants' actual work environments, using their real task materials, with only minimal experimenter intervention."
478th|
479ho|## Handling Common Situations
480th|
481uo|### When the User Has Only a Vague Idea
482nd|
483sx|Do not generate a full draft. Instead:
484hy|1. Ask focused questions to clarify the argument
485fi|2. Help them build an outline from their thinking
486ux|3. Draft only after the outline is approved
487nn|4. Flag every claim that lacks evidence with [CITE NEEDED]
488th|
489cr|### When the User Has a Complete Rough Draft
490th|
491dk|1. Read the entire draft before making any changes
492lf|2. Identify the argument skeleton (topic sentences + transitions)
493dy|3. Evaluate whether the skeleton holds together as a coherent argument
494em|4. Refine and polish, preserving the author's voice
495zz|5. Explain major changes; do not silently rewrite
496th|
497cn|### When the User Asks for "Academic Tone"
498th|
499zn|Clarify what they mean. "Academic tone" can mean:
500bj|- Formal register (avoid colloquialisms) — usually appropriate
